segments.s704-g297: همه جنگ را is the object phrase ('the whole/entire war') with را marking it as definite. تاختن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'assault/charge' and is linked to نوکنند: نوکنند (3rd plural, archaic-present/pluperfective sense here) means 'they make anew / they renew', so together تاختن نوکنند = 'they renewed (or made anew) the assault/charge' (SOV order: object before verb).
segments.s704-g298: برزم اندرون is an adverbial locative phrase ('in the inner hall/assembly') with اندرون 'inside/within'. یاد خسرو کنند is a light construction where یاد (a noun 'remembrance; commemoration') is the object and کنند (3rd pl. 'they do/make') is the verb: 'they made remembrance of Khosrow' = 'they commemorated Khosrow'.
segments.s704-g299: وزان is a connective adverb 'and/from that' linking this result to what preceded. رویِ بهرام (Bahrām's face) is the subject; شد is the past copular/verb 'became'; پر ز درد is an adjectival phrase 'full of pain' where پر = 'full (of)' and ز = 'from/because of', so the clause means 'Bahram's face became full of pain.'
segments.s704-g300: پشیمان شده is a compound predication: پشیمان (regretful) with شده ('has become' / 'became') yields 'he became regretful' or 'he was filled with remorse.' زان = 'from that / because of that' introduces the cause, and همه کارکرد ('all the deeds/actions') names what he regrets; word order places the cause-phrase after the adjectival predicate.
segments.s704-g289: نهان داشت is a light verb construction: نهان (adjective/noun 'hidden') + داشت (past of داشتن) functioning as 'kept/held (hidden)'. دارنده (the possessor/holder) follows داشت as the logical subject in this poetic order, and کارجهان (literally 'work of the world' = 'worldly affairs') names what belongs to the possessor. The verb داشت is simple past here, so the whole segment reads 'the possessor of the world's affairs kept (it) hidden.'
segments.s704-g290: برین is the contracted written form of بر این ('upon/to this' / 'to this'); it gives a dative/locative sense 'to this (one)'. بنده is 'servant' and گشت is past of گشتن here with the intransitive meaning 'became'. آشکارا (adverb) modifies گشت and نهان follows as a predicative adjective—poetically reversed order—so the clause means 'to this servant the hidden became manifest' (i.e. the secret was revealed to this servant).
segments.s704-g291: فریدون فرخ is a name with an epithet (Fereydun, the Fortunate). ندید is the simple past negative 'did not see' (ندید = 'he did not see'), followed by این 'this' as the direct object and به خواب 'in a dream' (به + خواب: idiomatically 'in/while dreaming'). Word order places temporal/clarifying phrase after the verb authoritatively in poetic diction.
segments.s704-g292: A negative listing introduced by نه ... نه ... نه: 'neither ... nor ... nor ...'. The three following items are names (Tur, Salm, Afrasiab); و is the coordinating 'and' between the second and third names. In this line the speaker denies that any of these figures appeared in the dream.
segments.s704-g293: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'. امروز من دیدم is straightforward: امروز 'today', من 'I', دیدم past 'I saw'. ای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing a group; سرکشان is a poetic plural form meaning 'the proud / the rebellious' (vocative). The phrase thus reads 'that which I saw today, O proud ones' with the vocative inserted for address.
segments.s704-g294: ز is the poetic/genitive preposition 'of/from' introducing what follows as the content of the vision. پیروزی و شهریاری = 'victory and kingship/sovereignty' (two coordinated nouns). نشان is the head noun placed after them meaning 'sign' or 'token' (typical Persian poetic order: genitives before the head noun). So the whole segment is 'a sign of victory and of sovereignty.'
segments.s704-g295: بدیشان ('to them') is the prepositional phrase introducing the indirect object. بگفت is the past-tense narrative 'he said/told'. آن کجا دید شاه is an embedded interrogative clause with object/particle آن 'that', question word کجا 'where', دید 'saw', and شاه 'the king' as subject placed after the verb (a common poetic/order variation). The whole reads 'He said to them, "Where did the king see that?"'.
segments.s704-g296: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / from then on'. به before فرمود is a prepositional/stylistic marker often used with verbs of speech/command; فرمود here carries the sense of giving an order (past tense). تا in this poetic context functions as a purposive/subordinating particle 'so that / in order that' leading into or pointing at the target 'آن سپاه' ('that army'). The segment therefore means 'Thereafter he commanded (so) to that army' (i.e. he gave orders to that army).
segments.s704-g129: دگر here is a discourse adverb meaning 'another/then' pointing to a new group; گرد is the noun 'troop/host' (classical epic uses گرد for an army) attached to the name شاپور by juxtaposition (equivalent to 'the host of Shapur'); با + اندیان is a prepositional phrase 'with the Indians' modifying that host. Word order is noun + possessor (no ezâfe) as is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s704-g130: چو introduces a simile 'as/like'; بند is a noun 'bond/fetter' here used metaphorically and takes the resumptive pronoun وی ('him/it') as its possessive/object; و links the next image; گردوی is an adjectival/attributive form 'walnut-colored' modifying پشت 'back', and کیان is the plural noun 'the Kians/kings'. The sequence gives two parallel images introduced by چو.
segments.s704-g131: چو marks a simile 'as/like'; آذرگشسپ is a proper name (a hero); و links a second item; دگر شیر means 'another lion' or 'a second lion' and ذیل here means 'below/under' or 'in the train of' — i.e. placing that lion in relation to the previous name. The structure lists parallel heroic images.
segments.s704-g132: Again چو begins a simile; زنگوی (a personal name) is followed by the adjective گستاخ in postpositive position (noun + adjective = 'Zangui, the audacious'); با introduces the comitative phrase 'with lion and elephant' (شیر و پیل) — a straightforward noun phrase describing his attendants/companions in battle.
segments.s704-g133: تخواره is an epithet or name functioning as the subject; که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; در جنگ is a locative prepositional phrase 'in battle'; غمخواره is an adjectival/participial description 'sorrow-stricken' or 'mourning'; بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause describes the subject's state in battle.
segments.s704-g134: یلان is the plural noun 'the heroes'; سینه را is the object marked by را ('their chests' / 'the chests'); زشت is an adjective 'ugly' modifying the following noun; پتیاره is an obscure/archaismsubstantive whose precise meaning is uncertain here (it is the noun being characterized as ugly); بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase states that the heroes' chests bore that ugly thing/state.
segments.s704-g135: فرخ‌زاد is a proper name as first item; و links the following comparison; چون introduces a simile 'like'; خسرو here is a name/title (Khosrow) and سرفراز is a postpositive adjective 'sarfraz' meaning 'victorious/proud'. The sequence puts Farakhzād alongside the famed, proud Khosrow by comparison.
segments.s704-g136: چو again marks a simile; اشتاد is used as a proper name here (a heroic figure); پیروز is an adjective 'victorious' modifying that name; دشمن گداز is a verbless compound in which گداز (literally 'melting/ardor') is used metaphorically to mean 'destroyer/scourge of enemies' — classical Persian often forms agentive or evocative compounds this way. The whole phrase presents Ashtad as a victorious enemy‑crusher.
segments.s704-g137: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. فرخنده خورشید is a poetic epithet 'the auspicious sun' (adjective before noun). با + اورمزد marks accompaniment 'with Ahura Mazda' and sits after the subject, a common poetic word order where the circumstantial phrase follows the noun.
segments.s704-g138: که introduces the subordinate content of the temporal clause ('such that' / 'so that' / 'that'); دشمن بدی is the agent + abstract object 'the enemy (and) evil' and پیش ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of them'. فرزد here functions as a verb-like action in the poem (meaning 'dare/attempt/show'), not the nominal sense given in the glossary, so its lexical senses do not match the usage.
segments.s704-g139: چو here is fused with مردان as a poetic formula meaning 'as/when (it came to) men' or simply 'he (as) men chose'; گزین کرد is a compound verbal expression 'chose' (گزین = choose, کرد = did/made) with ز ایران marking source 'from Iran'. دو هفت is a numeric expression 'two sevens' (poetic way to say fourteen).
segments.s704-g140: ز لشکر marks origin 'from the army/host'; بیک سو is 'toward the bey' or 'the bey toward (a direction)' with سو meaning 'toward/side', and خرامید is the past tense 'strode/pranced proudly'. تفت here appears to be a place-name or a term not matching the provided glosses (which list cooking senses), so its provided senses do not apply.
segments.s704-g141: چنین پیشposes the manner 'thus/so' and is followed by گفت (here the verb 'said'); word order places manner before verb. خسرو is the subject 'Khosrow'. بدین مهتران is the indirect object 'to these chiefs/leaders' formed from به ('to') + این ('these') plus مهتران 'chiefs' — the poem spells/segments these elements unconventionally.
segments.s704-g142: که‌ای is a vocative formula (که + ای) used to call out; here it introduces direct address. سرفرازن is a plural/collective epithet 'the triumphant ones' used vocatively, و is the coordinating 'and', and فرمانبران is another epithet 'those who bear/obey commands' or 'commanders/obedient ones' — all in a vocative list addressed by the king.
segments.s704-g143: همه is 'all/everyone' and پشت را is the direct object with را (literally 'the backs'). سوی یزدان کنید is an imperative phrase 'turn/toward God' where سوی means 'toward' and کنید is the 2nd-person plural imperative of کردن 'do/make' used here with a motion sense ('turn'). The clause is idiomatic: 'turn your backs to God' = entrust/commit yourselves to God.
segments.s704-g144: دل خویش را is 'your own heart' where دل 'heart' + خوی 'own/disposition' + ش (clitic) yields 'your heart' and را marks it as object. شاد و خندان کنید is a coordinated predicate 'make (them) glad and cheerful' with کنید again the imperative. The adjective pair shad و khandan expresses joyful, smiling cheer.
segments.s704-g113: بدیدم is a past‑tense 1st‑person verb 'I saw'. It takes the following nominal phrase as its object: هنرهای رومی همه 'all the Roman arts/skills'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (verb up front instead of the more prosaic SOV).
segments.s704-g114: بسان introduces a simile 'like/as'. رمه روزگار دمه is a compact image: 'a flock (رمه) of (or belonging to) time/season (روزگار) of a moment/breath (دمه)', i.e. something transient. The noun phrase functions as the complement of the simile.
segments.s704-g115: هم آن به که is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'it is better that' or 'better to...'; it introduces a clause. The remainder 'من با سپاه اندکی' is the clause's subject phrase 'I, with a small army/host', where با + noun marks accompaniment or instrument.
segments.s704-g116: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (equivalent to modern از). چوبینه is adjectival/qualifying 'wooden' (i.e. made of wood) and modifies the thing to be brought. آورد خواهم is a slightly inverted verbal cluster where آورد 'bring' and خواهم (future/auxiliary 'I will') together express 'I will bring'; poetic word order places the lexical verb before the auxiliary. یکی is the direct object 'one'.
segments.s704-g117: نخواهم is the negative of the desire/auxiliary 'I will not / I do not want'. درین کار is 'in this affair/this matter' (درین = در + این, a poetic fusion). یاری ز کس 'help from anyone' is the object; the phrase expresses refusal to seek assistance from others.
segments.s704-g118: امیدم is 'my hope' (امید + enclitic -م). به + یزدان marks the locus/recipient of that hope 'in/unto God', and فریادرس is an appositive epithet 'rescuing helper' — altogether 'my hope is in God the rescuer'.
segments.s704-g119: بدو 'to him' is an indirect-object prepositional form ('to/unto him'). The clause should be read as 'گستهم گفت بدو' (poetic inversion): گستهم (a proper name, Gostaham) is the speaker, گفت is the verb 'said', and کای شهریار is the vocative 'O king'. کای is a fixed vocative marker used before titles.
segments.s704-g120: به شیرین روانت is a prepositional phrase addressing the addressee's 'sweet soul' (شیرین = 'sweet', روانت = 'your soul' with enclitic -ت). مخور is the negative imperative 'do not consume / do not give [up]' and زینهار is an emphatic admonition 'beware' or 'take heed', closing the admonitory address.
segments.s704-g233: بخسرو is the prepositional phrase 'بِ + خسرو' contracted, meaning 'to the king' and functions as the indirect object before the verb. چنین گفت is the narrative phrase 'thus said' (literally 'said thus'); گفت is the past tense reporting verb ('said') and کای پرفریب is a vocative (کای = 'O') addressing someone as 'پرفریب' ('deceptive'/'crafty'). Word order reflects classical/poetic Persian: indirect object + adverb + verb, then the direct address.
segments.s704-g234: This is a poetic inversion: the locative phrase 'به پیش فراز' ('toward/in front of the summit/height') precedes the verb توآمد ('came'), and نشیب ('the descent') follows as the thing that arrived. توآمد is an older past-form of آمدن ('came'); فراز/نشیب form the antonymic pair 'rise and fall' (فراز و نشیب). The clause means roughly 'to the front of the height came the descent' (i.e. the decline arrived before the rise).
segments.s704-g235: برمن ('بر + من') means 'at/on me' (i.e. 'against me'). چراتاختی is a fused colloquial/poetic sequence = چرا تاختی 'why did you charge/attack?', where تاختی is 2nd-person singular past 'you charged'. هوش خویش is 'your wits/intellect' formed by هوش + خویش (خوی + ش) with ش as the enclitic possessive — together meaning 'your own intelligence'.
segments.s704-g236: نهاده is the past participial/past form 'placed/put' (here in active sense 'you have put'); بر این گونه is the phrase 'in this manner' (بر + این(=ین) + گونه), where ین is the orthographic/poetic form of این. بردوش is the compound 'بر دوش' ('on the shoulder'); خویش (خوی + ش) again supplies the reflexive/possessive 'your own'. Together this describes a figurative action: 'having placed your wits on your shoulder in this way' (i.e. carrying them but not using them).
segments.s704-g237: چوشد is the fused poetic form 'چو شد' = 'when it became/when it happened'. زان = 'from that' (referring back to the previous sign), and نشان کار is a noun phrase 'sign (نشان) [of] the affair/affair (کار)'. برشاه تنگ is a predicative construction: کار (the affair) becomes تنگ ('tight, constrained') for the king (بر شاه). The whole clause means 'when from that sign the affair became tight for the king.'
segments.s704-g238: This is a verbless, picturesque listing of the king's predicament: پس ('then') introduces the result; پشت شمشیر = 'a sword at (his) back' (پشت used as a locative), و = 'and', در پیش سنگ = 'and a rock in front (of him)' (در + پیش used locatively). The line omits a copular verb, a common poetic device, to show the king trapped between sword and rock.
segments.s704-g239: به یزدان is the dative/prepositional phrase 'to God' (به + یزدان). چنین گفت again places the adverb 'thus' before the verb 'said' (چنین + گفت). کای کردگار is a vocative: کای 'O' addressing God directly, followed by کردگار 'the Maker/Creator' as the vocative noun.
segments.s704-g240: توی here functions as an older/poetic form addressing the addressee ('thou/you are' or 'in you'); برتر از is the comparative construction 'higher/superior than' (برتر + از introducing the standard of comparison). گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase 'the turning/revolution of time' i.e. 'the vicissitudes/cycles of fate'. Together: '[You are] above the turning of time.'
segments.s704-g65: سپر برسرآورد is a compact past action: سپر (shield) is the object and برسرآورد (literally “put on the head / brought onto the head”) is a past tense compound verb meaning ‘lifted/placed (the) shield over (his) head.’ The following وشمشیر تیز uses the conjunction و (written together here as وشمشیر) to link a second object; the verb is understood to govern both actions (i.e. ‘lifted the shield and [drew] a sharp sword’). تیز modifies شمشیر (‘sharp’).
segments.s704-g66: برآورد here functions as a past tense verb meaning ‘brought forth / caused to arise’ (a slightly oblique or poetic use of برآورد). زان is a contracted/poetic form meaning ‘from that / from those,’ introducing source. جنگیان (plural) names the combatants/war-like people connected to the source, and رستخیز is a noun meaning an uprising/tumult — so the clause says that from those warriors a ruckus/uprising arose.
segments.s704-g67: پیاده here is used as a plural animate noun ‘the foot‑soldiers/infantry’ (not merely the adverb ‘on foot’). زبهرام (= ز + بهرام) is the source preposition ‘from Bahram.’ The verb بگریختند is a past plural (‘they fled’) agreeing with پیاده; word order is typical Persian: topic/subject + source phrase + verb at the end.
segments.s704-g68: کمانهای چاچی is a genitive/possessive construction (‘the bows of Chači / Chaachi’s bows’); the ezāfe is not written but the relation is clear. فروریختند is a perfective past plural verb ‘collapsed / were shattered / fell apart,’ agreeing with the plural subject کمانهای. The line thus reports the bows breaking/collapsing.
segments.s704-g69: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘at one time / all at once / suddenly’ (یکی = one, باره = occasion/instance). بردند is a past plural verb ‘they carried/took (away)’; هم here adds the sense ‘also/even’ or ‘at the same time.’ درزمان is a temporal adverbial ‘in that moment / at that time,’ so the whole chunk gives the suddenness/timing of the action.
segments.s704-g70: سپهبد is the subject/title ‘the spahbed (general).’ نشست is the simple past of نشستن here meaning ‘sat down / took a seat’ (it can also mean ‘held a session’ in other contexts). از بر is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning ‘away from / apart from / out of’ and governs the following noun اودمان, which here denotes the household/retinue/party (a somewhat dialectal/poetic word). The clause therefore says the spahbed sat apart from that group.
segments.s704-g71: خروشان is an adverbial participle ‘roaring / tumultuously’ describing how they moved. همی‌تاخت is an old/poetic imperfective past form with the prefix همی- marking continuous or iterative action; تاخت is ‘to charge/ride’ so همی‌تاخت = ‘they were charging / charged.’ تا introduces the extent/destination (‘as far as’), and قلبگاه is the destination ‘the heart / center (of the battle).’
segments.s704-g72: بجایی here functions as a locative phrase ‘to the place / to the very spot’; کجا is an emphatic/deictic ‘where/that spot where.’ شاه is the noun ‘the king.’ بد is an attributive adjective meaning ‘ill / in a bad state’ in this context, and بی‌سپاه is a compound adjective ‘without an army / unescorted / defenseless.’ The sequence therefore describes the place where the king lay ill and without troops.
segments.s704-g225: This phrase gives a locative starting point. بن (here) means 'at the base / mouth' of something and precedes the noun as in classical Persian. غارهم is the cave (غاره with the enclitic هم 'also' attached), and بسته آمد is a two-word predicate where بسته is an adjectival/state word 'closed/compact' that occurs before the verb آمد 'came' to yield 'came ... (in a) closed/compact fashion' or 'came to the (closed) mouth'; زکوه is a prepositional adverbial 'from the mountain' (ز + کوه).
segments.s704-g226: بماند is the past form of ماندن 'remain' (here 'remained'); the verb precedes the subject for emphasis. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying جهاندار 'sovereign/king'. دور ازگروه is the predicate complement: دور 'distant' plus ازگروه 'from the group/host', indicating separation from the company.
segments.s704-g227: فرود آمد is a verbal compound meaning 'alighted / dismounted' (فرود = descent/alighting, آمد = came/occurred). از + اسپ is the source phrase 'from the horse/steed'. فرخ جوان here are epithets for the steed (literally 'fortunate/bright' and 'young'), following the noun in classical descriptive order.
segments.s704-g228: This clause describes his movement after dismounting. پیاده functions adverbially 'on foot'; بران (here 'بر آن') is spatial 'onto/onto that' linking to کوه 'mountain'; برشد is a past verbal form 'rose/climbed up' (literally 'it/he rose up'); دوان is a present participial/adverbial sense 'running' or 'in haste', so the sequence yields 'on foot he ran up the mountain.' Word order is emphatic/poetic: adverbial + place + verb + manner.
segments.s704-g229: پیاده شد is a simple past 'he became on foot' i.e. 'he dismounted / went on foot.' و راه is the conjunctive 'and the way/road' (و attached to راه). اوبسته شد is a passive-like construction where اوبسته ('closed to him') plus شد yields 'the way was closed to him' or 'the route was blocked against him' (او = him).
segments.s704-g230: This is a straightforward nominal-predicate sentence: دل نامداران 'the hearts of the renowned (men/heroes)' is the subject phrase; ازو 'because of him / from him' gives the cause or object of feeling; خسته شد is the past 'became tired/wearied', so 'the renowned's hearts became weary of him.' Note the classical order subject + circumstantial phrase + predicate.
segments.s704-g231: نه ... ونه is the classical correlative 'neither ... nor.' جای درنگ 'place for delay' and جای گریز 'place for flight/escape' are parallel noun phrases: جای 'space/room' + abstract noun (درنگ 'delay', گریز 'flight'), giving 'no room to delay and no room to flee.'
segments.s704-g232: پس اندر همی‌رفت is a temporal/locative sequence: پس 'then', اندر 'into/among', همی‌رفت is the imperfect/past-continuous form 'was going / went into the midst.' The subject بهرام تیز follows for emphasis: بهرام is the name, تیز an epithet 'swift', so 'then Bahram the swift went in among them.'
segments.s704-g193: برفتند is a 3rd-person plural past verb (they departed/went) placed before an explicit subject (omitted here). زان is a classical prepositional demonstrative 'from that'. رزمگه is the noun 'battlefield'. سوی + noun (سوی کوه) is the directional phrase 'toward the mountain'.
segments.s704-g194: که introduces a relative/clause of place here ('where' / 'which'). دیدار is a noun 'meeting' or 'rendezvous'. بودی is the past copula 'was' (classical past). بهر دو گروه = 'for the two groups' with بهر meaning 'for/on behalf of' and گروه meaning 'troop/party'.
segments.s704-g195: همی‌گفت is a habitual/imperfective past form 'kept saying' or 'would say' used to report repeated speech. هرکس که introduces a relative clause 'whoever/anyone who'. پر + مایه is a compound where پر = 'full (of)' and مایه = 'resource/means', so پرمایه here means 'one who has the king's resources' or 'the king's man/favorite'. شاه is the noun 'king' modifying the phrase.
segments.s704-g196: چرا is a rhetorical interrogative 'why'. جان is the direct object 'life'. فروشد is the verb 'to sell' used idiomatically here ('sell/give up one's life'); the provided lemma senses did not include the verb meaning, so no matching index is available. ز بهر is the classical prepositional phrase 'for/on account of' and کلاه literally 'hat' (here meaning helmet/cap or a small prize), the thing for which the life would supposedly be sold.
segments.s704-g197: بماند is a past/imperfective verb 'remained' (3rd-person singular/plural depending on context). بدین here functions as 'in/at this' (ب = prepositional 'to/in', دین represents 'this' in the fused form بدین; the dataset split it but the compound means 'in this'). دشت is 'plain' and چندین سوار = 'several horsemen' (چندین = several, سوار = rider/horseman).
segments.s704-g198: شود is the 3rd-person singular of شدن 'become'/'stand' used here to indicate a resultant state ('(one) stood / became'). خیره is an adjectival/participial form meaning 'staring' (fixed gaze). تنها means 'alone' or 'only'. سوی کارزار is the directional phrase 'toward the battlefield'. Together they describe someone left standing alone, staring at the battle.
segments.s704-g199: همه is 'all/everyone'. دست برآسمان داشتند literally 'had hands to the sky' = 'raised their hands to heaven' (a gesture of prayer/imploring). داشتند is the past form of داشتن ('they had' / here functioning as the main past verb describing their action).
segments.s704-g200: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. او را is the object-marked pronoun 'him' (را marks definite object). همه modifies پنداشتند 'they all supposed/imagined'. کشته is a past participle/adjective 'killed' or 'slain' describing his state; پنداشتند is the past-tense verb 'they thought/supposed'. The clause therefore means 'that they all supposed him killed.'
segments.s704-g153: بکردند پیمان is a past-tense clause: بکردند (they made/did) + پیمان (a pact) = 'they made a pact'. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows. از شهریار is a prepositional/partitive phrase 'of/from the king' which will link to the indefinite noun (کسی) in the next segment; in classical Persian such modifiers may appear before or after the noun they qualify.
segments.s704-g154: کسی is the indefinite subject 'anyone/no one' and برنگردد is a negative present/subjunctive-form (classical usage with بر- prefix) meaning 'may not return' or 'shall not return'. ازین is the older orthography of 'از این' = 'from this', where -ین is the clitic/variant of این, and کارزار is the object/place 'battle/field'. Together: 'that no one should return from this battle.'
segments.s704-g155: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander'. بشنید is simple past third-person 'he heard'. The coordinate ו (and) links to آرام یافت, where آرام is a predicative noun/adjective 'calm/peace' and یافت is past 'he found/obtained' — idiomatically 'he was reassured / became calm.' Word order is typical classical Persian: subject + past verb, then coordinated clause.
segments.s704-g156: خوش آمدش is an impersonal/experiential construction: خوش آمد (it pleased) + enclitic ـش 'to him' = 'it pleased him'. وز here is the fused form of و + ز (and + from) linking the next prepositional idea. مهتران (the leaders/chiefs) is the source, and کام یافت is a fixed classical phrase 'his desire was fulfilled / he obtained satisfaction' with یافت as past 'obtained'.
segments.s704-g157: سپه رابه is the object-marked phrase 'the army' (سپه + object marker را) plus the preposition به attached: classical orthography often fuses را + به as رابه before the indirect object. Thus the verb سپرد (he entrusted/handed over) takes سپه رابه as its direct object and بهرام فرخ is the named recipient ('to Bahram Farrokh').
segments.s704-g158: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/past-iterative form 'he was going / set out' (همی‑- classical continuous marker). با چارده مرد is a simple prepositional phrase 'with fourteen men'. گرد here functions adverbially/nominatively to indicate a small company or 'around/about' (classical usage implying a retinue or band), so the whole phrase means 'he departed with a company of fourteen men.'
segments.s704-g159: هم آنگه is a temporal expression 'then / at that moment' (هم adding emphasis). خروش آمد is a compound where خروش 'a cry/clamor' + آمد 'came/rose' = 'a cry rose'. از دیده‌گاه uses از (from) + دیده‌گاه 'field of sight/watchpoint/lookout' to indicate the source of the cry.
segments.s704-g160: به بهرام گفتند is a reporting clause 'they said to Bahram' (به + indirect object; گفتند = they said). کامد سپاه is the reported content: کامد is an archaic/polite present-form 'comes / is coming' and سپاه 'the army' is the logical subject. Classical poetic order often places the verb before its noun for emphasis, so 'کامد سپاه' = 'the army is coming.'
segments.s704-g257: بدین زودی is an adverbial phrase meaning “in/with this shortness” = “so soon.” بدین is literary/archaic (به + این) and precedes the adverb زودی. اندر is the preposition “in/within,” and بشاهی is the noun ‘kingship’ (object/goal). رسی is 2nd‑person singular of رسیدن used here as a future/result (classical present/optative form) meaning “you will attain/reach.” Word order places the temporal adverbial before the verb.
segments.s704-g258: بدین سالیان = “in these years” (بدین as above + سالیان ‘years’). بگذرد is 3rd‑person singular of گذشتن used in this classical line to express that time will pass (future/result sense: “will pass”). هشت و سی (thirty‑eight) is the numeral that follows and names the duration. The conjunction/connector و appears inside the numeral phrase.
segments.s704-g259: بگفت is simple past 3rd‑person “he said.” این سخن is the direct object “these words,” with نیز adding “also.” و شد is a sequence marker “and then became,” where شد (become/happen) combines with the adjective ناپدید to mean “became vanished” or “vanished.” The clause is a compact past narrative: he said X and then disappeared.
segments.s704-g260: کس here functions with the negative verb ندید to mean “no one.” اندر جهان = “in the world.” این شگفتی is the definite object “this wonder/marvel.” ندید is past of دیدن in its negative form (did not see) — together the clause says that nobody in the world had seen such a marvel.
segments.s704-g261: چو = “when.” چو آن دید بهرام is an inverted, classical word order where the temporal clause (when that he saw) precedes the subject: “when Bahram saw that.” خیره بماند is a compound predicate: خیره “staring/dazed” (adjective/adverbial state) + بماند (from ماندن) meaning “he remained” or “stood” in that state — a stative/past resulting action.
segments.s704-g262: جهان‌آفرین را is the definite object marked by را (here `to/for the Creator` as the addressee of his calling). فراوان is an adverb “many times/abundantly,” modifying the verb. بخواند (here 3rd‑person past narrative form) means “he called/prayed/summoned” — together: “he called repeatedly upon the Creator.”
segments.s704-g263: همی‌گفت is the habitual/past iterative form “he kept saying.” تا introduces a temporal clause here meaning “as long as/until.” جنگ مردم بود = “there was war among the people” (جنگ = war; مردم = the people; بود is the past copula ‘was’), so the clause states the time-span during which he kept repeating the statement.
segments.s704-g264: مبادا is an optative/negative formula meaning “may it not be / God forbid / lest.” که introduces the subordinate clause. مردی literally ‘a man’ (here meaning ‘one of my men’), ز من = “from me/from my side,” گم is the adjective “lost/absent,” and بود is the copula ‘was’ — together expressing the wish that none of his men be lost to him.
segments.s704-g73: همه (modifier) precedes the noun and here means 'the whole/entire' qualifying قلبگه؛ قلبگه is a compound noun 'seat/place of the heart'. پاک is an adjective after قلبگه (typical Persian adjective order) meaning 'pure'. برهم is an adverb/adjectival predicate meaning 'in disorder/into pieces' and combines with the past verb درید ('was torn') to form a passive/eventive result: literally 'the whole pure heart-seat was torn to pieces.'
segments.s704-g74: درفش جهاندار is a noun phrase in apposition/juxtaposition (no ezāfe written) meaning 'the standard/banner (درفش) of the world‑holder/sovereign (جهاندار)'. شد is the past of شدن used intransitively as 'became' and is followed by the adjective ناپدید 'vanished', so the clause reads 'the sovereign's banner became vanished.' This is a poetic/older Persian diction but grammatically modern: noun phrase + شد + adjective.
segments.s704-g75: وزان is a fused prepositional phrase (= و + ز آن) meaning 'and/from that' or simply 'from that'; it sets a temporal/spatial reference for the clause. جایگه is a noun 'place/position'. شد is 'became' and سوی میسره is a small prepositional phrase: سوی = 'toward/way to' and میسره (adjective) means 'attainable/accessible', so together 'the place became accessible' (i.e. the approach became possible).
segments.s704-g76: پس is a discourse adverb meaning 'then/thereafter'. پشتش is پشت + enclitic ش (his/its) and here means 'his rear/behind' (پشت = rear/behind). آزادگان is a plural noun 'the freemen/nobles' and یکسره is an adverb/adjective meaning 'completely/entirely', so the clause says 'then his rear was entirely freemen' (i.e. his rear‑guard was wholly composed of freemen).
segments.s704-g77: نگهبان is the subject 'the guard/warden'. آن is the demonstrative 'that' modifying دست; دست here is a noun 'hand' used in a descriptive epithet (in older Persian physical epithets like 'walnut‑handed' describe complexion or arm). گردوی is an adjectival form 'walnut‑colored' (not the fruit) describing دست. بود is the past copula 'was'. Together: 'The guard of that post was (a) walnut‑complexioned (man).' (Poetic epithet; the exact referent is 'the guard' described as 'walnut‑handed'.)
segments.s704-g78: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing a relative clause; مردی is 'a man' (predicate nominal), دلیر an adjective 'brave', and وجهانجوی is a compound/adjective meaning 'and world‑seeking' or 'seeker of the world' (i.e. ambitious/conqueror‑seeking). بود is the past copula 'was'. Grammatically this is a standard relative clause giving more information about the guard: 'who was a brave and world‑seeking man.'
segments.s704-g79: This is a compact nominative + object + verb line: برادر چوروی = 'the thief's brother' (possessive/genitive relation: noun + modifier), followed by برادر (here likely 'the brother' being seen) and بدید, past of دیدن 'saw'. Word order is Subject (برادر چوروی) then Object (برادر) then Verb (بدید) — typical Persian flexibility where constituents can appear in this order for emphasis.
segments.s704-g80: کمان را uses را as the definite/accusative marker on the object 'the bow'. بزه here appears as a verbal or verbalized element whose literal gloss in the provided senses doesn't match the context (classical usage implies 'seize/lay hold of' or perform an action upon), and it combines with کرد ('did/made') to form a compound verbal idea 'took hold of / handled the bow'. واندرکشید is a poetic/older compound verb (و + اندرکشید = 'and pulled/drew in') in the simple past, meaning 'and drew it (in/back)'.
segments.s704-g17: بدست (badast) here functions as a locative prepositional phrase 'in/on the hand (of)'; چپش (chap-ash) is 'his left (side/hand)' (چپ + pronominal -ش), so the phrase says 'on his left (hand)'. نامدار (namdār) is an adjective 'renowned/famed' that directly modifies the following noun (ارمنی). Word order is possessor/locative + modifier + noun, typical of Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s704-g18: This segment lists the equipment or attribute associated with that left hand. جوشن (jūshan) 'cuirass/armor' and وتیغ (va-tīgh) 'and sword' (وتیغ = و + تیغ) form a coordinate noun phrase; آهرمنی (āhramanī) is an adjectival qualifier 'Ahriman‑like, demonic' that qualifies the armor/sword. ابا is anomalous here in the provided senses; in the manuscript tradition an initial ا sometimes appears before با ('with') or as an exclamatory particle, so its exact function is uncertain — I mark its sense as not applicable. The conjunction و is fused to تیغ in the surface form, so وتیغ should be read as 'and (the) sword.'
segments.s704-g19: مبارز (mobārez) is a participial/nominal agent 'the warrior/fighter'. چوشاپور (Chushāpūr) here functions as a proper name or epithet identifying that fighter. وچون (va-chun) is the conjunction + comparative/temporal 'and as/and like', linking this subject to what follows, and اندیان (andiyān) is an archaic/poetic third‑person plural copula/ending (from اند) meaning 'they are/they were', used to mark 'and such were (they)'. Together this segment names the fighter and adds 'and (so) were others like him'.
segments.s704-g20: بران (barān) here functions as a discourse connective 'thereupon/thereon' or 'upon that'; جنگ (jang) is the noun 'battle/war'; بر (bar) is the preposition 'on/against/in' introducing the location; تنگ (tang) is an adjective 'narrow' and بسته (baste) 'closed/fastened/enclosed' modifies تنگ to give a compound idea 'narrow and enclosed'; میان (miyān) is a postpositional noun 'between'. Grammatically this yields 'thereupon the fighting was on/in a narrow, closed pass between (them)'.
segments.s704-g21: همی‌بود (hamī-būd) is the imperfective/classical continuous past copula 'was / continued to be'. گستهم (Gostaham) in the Shahnameh is the proper name Gostaham (despite the lemma's verbal appearance) and here is the subject; بردست شاه (be-dast-e shāh) is a genitival/locative phrase 'in the hand of the king' or 'at the king's hand/side' meaning 'held by the king' or 'at the king's disposal'. The segment thus says 'Gostaham was at the king's hand/side.'
segments.s704-g22: This is a relative/explanatory clause. که (ke) is the relative particle 'who/that'; دارد (dārad) is the present/habitual 'has/keeps' (here stative 'keeps/holds'); مر (mar) is the archaic/object pronoun 'me' (to/for me); او (ū) is 'him' (the referent of the relative clause); را is the object marker; ز (ze) is the poetic preposition 'from'; دشمن (doshaman) 'enemy'; نگاه (negāh) 'gaze/sight'. Literally: 'who keeps him for me/from the enemy's gaze' — naturally rendered 'for he keeps me safe from the enemy's sight' or 'who keeps him away from the enemy's gaze for me.' The pronoun ordering and را reflect classical Persian word order and emphasis.
segments.s704-g23: چوبهرام (chūb-e Bahrām) can be read as an instrumental/epithet 'Bahram's club' or a kenning; in practice it marks the weapon/instrument. یل (yil/yal) is 'hero' and رومیان (rūmiyān) is the direct object 'the Romans'; راندید (rāndīd) is a past tense verb 'drove away/put to flight'. Word order places the instrumental/epithet first for poetic effect, then the agent noun and patient and the past verb, yielding 'with Bahram's club the hero drove the Romans off.'
segments.s704-g24: درنگی (daran-gī / derangi) is a noun 'a pause' (or 'a hesitation'); شد (shod) is the past of شدن 'became/occurred' — so 'a pause occurred'; وخامشی (va-khāmoshī) = و + خامشی 'and silence' (خامشی = 'silence'); برگزید (bargozīd) is a past tense verb 'chose/selected'. Together: 'a pause occurred, and (he) chose silence.' The sequence uses nominal + شد to state that a state came about, followed by the conjunctive and a chosen reaction.
segments.s704-g57: زپیکان is the fused prepositional phrase ز + پیکان ('by/from the arrows') placed before the verb phrase in typical Persian word order; چنان گشت is 'became so' (گشت = became, sense of change) and خرطوم پیل is a noun phrase 'the elephant's trunk' (خرطوم = elephant's trunk, پیل = elephant). Together it means 'the elephant's trunk became like that/from the arrows.'
segments.s704-g58: توگفتی is a reporting clause 'you said' (past). شد is the past form 'became' (here introducing the reported result), and از خستگی is a causal phrase 'from/because of fatigue.' پیل نیل is a noun phrase where پیل = 'elephant' and نیل qualifies it (here as a color/attribute). The whole second half reads as reported speech: 'you said the (Nile/dark) elephant became weary.'
segments.s704-g59: هم آنگاه is an emphatic temporal phrase 'at that very moment' (هم + آنگاه). بهرام is the subject. بالای here names 'the high place/height' and خواست is past 'sought/wanted' (خواست functioning as a verb of attempt/aim: 'he tried/attempted to gain the height').
segments.s704-g60: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one.' مغفر is a 'covering/helmet' (here a piece of armor), خسرو آرای is a compound-like description 'king‑adorned' or 'belonging among the king's ornaments' (خسرو qualifies آرای), and خواست (again) is 'he wished/asked for'—so 'he sought one of the king's ornamented helmets.'
segments.s704-g61: همان is 'that/same' pointing back to the previous action; تیرباران is a nominal compound 'shower of arrows.' گرفتند here has an inceptive/performative sense 'they took up/began' (rather than only 'seized'), and باز is the adverb 'again'—so 'they again began that same arrow‑shower.'
segments.s704-g62: برآشفت is a past intransitive verb 'became enraged/was agitated' (بر- prefix + آشفته root); بهرام is the subject. گردن‌فراز is a descriptive epithet 'proud, lofty‑necked' applied to Bahram. The clause reads 'Bahram, proud, was enraged.'
segments.s704-g63: پیاده شد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'he dismounted/was on foot' (پیاده + شد). آن مرد is the noun phrase 'that man,' and پرخاشخر is a postpositive adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive.' The sequence therefore means 'that quarrelsome man dismounted.'
segments.s704-g64: دامنش را زد بر کمر is compressed in the line as زره دامنش رابزد برکمر. زره 'armor' names the item; دامنش is دامن + ـش ('its/ his skirt/hem'); رابزد is the verb زد with the object marker را attached (poetically written together) meaning 'struck/fastened' and برکمر indicates the target location 'to/around his waist.' Idiomatically this means 'he girded the skirt/hem of his armor about his waist.'
segments.s704-g41: به نامه here uses به in an instrumental/adverbial sense 'by/with (the) letter'; توپاداش is an archaic fused form meaning 'as a reward'; یابی is a present/subjunctive/optative form 'you may obtain/you shall find' (verb follows the object phrase as is common in Persian), and زمن is the poetic preposition ز + pronoun من = 'from me'. The whole segment expresses that by the letter one may receive a reward from the speaker.
segments.s704-g42: هم is a particle meaning 'also/too' here; ازنامداران is a compound 'از + نامداران' meaning 'from/among the renowned (persons)' and functions as a nominal group; این انجمن 'this assembly/council' identifies the group being spoken of. The phrase as a whole appends that the reward concerns the notable persons of this assembly.
segments.s704-g43: چوهنگام is the temporal clause 'چو + هنگام' = 'when the time (is)'; باشد is a verbal form meaning 'be / is' used here to mark the subordinate time clause; بگویم is the first-person verb 'I will say / I will tell' (here used with subordinate clause meaning 'I shall tell'); تو را is the object 'you' marked by را (object/ topical marker). Word order places the verb before the object for emphasis.
segments.s704-g44: زاندیشه is the fused prepositional phrase 'ز + اندیشه' = 'from/out of (the) thought' or 'of thought'; بد qualifies اندیشه as 'evil / bad thought'; بشویم here functions transitively in this context meaning 'I will rid/cleanse' (an older/poetic usage — the supplied sense list does not include 'cleanse/remove', so the precise lexical mapping is irregular); تو را is again the object 'you' with را. The clause means the speaker will free you from bad thought.
segments.s704-g45: This token is actually the fused sequence 'چو بهرام' = 'when Bahram'; in the manuscript here the conjunction چو ('when') plus the proper name بهرام appear together. شنید is the past-tense verb 'he heard', and آواز خسرو is the object phrase 'the voice of Khosrow' (possessive/genitive relationship). The clause is a simple temporal clause: 'When Bahram heard Khosrow's voice...'.
segments.s704-g46: باندیشه = 'با + اندیشه' ('in/with thought', 'in his mind'); آن جادوی را = 'that magic' with را marking the definite object; بدید is the past tense 'he saw/perceived'. Together the phrase says that, in his thought/contemplation, he perceived/seen that sorcery.
segments.s704-g47: برآشفت is a past-form verbal compound meaning 'he became angry/was enraged'; وزان is 'و + زِ آن' = 'and from that / and because of that'; کار تنگ آمدش is an idiomatic phrase 'the affair became tight for him' meaning the situation grew difficult or constrained for him, with آمدش 'came to him' closing the clause. The sequence shows cause and consequence: his anger made the situation difficult.
segments.s704-g48: چوارغنده is an adjectival/poetic expression 'fourfold' (an intensifier here); شد is 'became'; رای is 'opinion/plan/counsel'; جنگ آمدش is a small clause 'war came to him' meaning 'he resolved on war' (i.e. his counsel turned toward war). Put together: his counsel became intensified/fourfold and he adopted war as his decision.
segments.s704-g273: This is a subordinate temporal clause: subject نیاطوس (Niatōs) + subordinating چـون meaning 'when/as' + object phrase روی خسرو 'Khosrow's face' and the verb ندید in the simple past negative 'did not see'. Word order is Subject — subordinator — object — verb, a common classical Persian ordering for a temporal clause.
segments.s704-g274: عماری زرین is a noun phrase 'a golden building/pavilion' (عماری = a dwelling/edifice, زرین = golden). به یکسو کشید is a verbal phrase with به + direction (یکسو 'to one side') followed by کشید, past of کشیدن 'to pull / draw / move'; the construction means 'he pulled/moved (it) to one side.'
segments.s704-g275: بمریم is a prepositional phrase 'to Maryam' (the dative). چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' in past). کاندر نشین is an imperative/instructional locative expression 'sit/stay therein' (کاندر = 'in that/therein', نشین = 'sit/settle').
segments.s704-g276: This is a single complex clause: the first که functions as a conjunction introducing the reason 'for' (because/I fear), ترسم is present 'I fear', the second که introduces the subordinate clause 'that the king of Iran ...', شد is past of 'become/happen' and here signals that something has befallen the king. ایران‌زمین is the compound 'land of Iran.'
segments.s704-g277: هم آنگاه is an emphatic temporal phrase 'at that very moment' (هم adds emphasis, آنگاه = then). خسرو is the subject. بران + روی کوه = 'on that (side/face) of the mountain' (بران = 'upon that / thereon', روی کوه = 'the mountain's face/side').
segments.s704-g278: پدید آمد is a compound verb 'appeared / came into view' (پدید = 'appear', آمد = 'came'). از راه دور is a prepositional phrase 'from a distant road' (از = from, راه = road/way, دور = distant). از گروه here indicates accompaniment or company 'with his troop/company' (literally 'from the group' but used as 'with the group').
segments.s704-g279: همه لشکر نامور is a noun phrase 'the whole / entire famed army' (همه = all/the whole, لشکر = army, نامور = renowned). شاد شد is a past-tense resultive 'became glad / rejoiced' (شاد = glad, شد = became).
segments.s704-g280: دل مریم is the possessive noun phrase 'Maryam's heart.' از درد آزاد شد is 'was freed from pain/sorrow' (از = from, درد = here emotional sorrow/anguish, آزاد = free, شد = became/was). The structure is passive/resultative: 'her heart became free of sorrow.'
segments.s704-g49: جفا پیشه is a compound: جفا (cruelty) + پیشه (one who practices), functioning as a substantive epithet 'one who practices cruelty' or 'the cruel one.' برپیل is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + پیل/«برِ پیل») meaning 'on (the) elephant' and acts adverbially. تنها is an adverb/adjectival modifier 'alone' and modifies the following verb. برفت is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'went/rode' (بر- + رفت), third person singular; the subject is the preceding epithet.
segments.s704-g50: سوی is a preposition meaning 'toward' and introduces the goal; قلب خسرو is a possessive/genitive sequence (quasi‑Ezāfe) 'the heart of Khosrow' where the possessor follows the possessed in classical style. خرامید is the simple past (3rd sg.) of خرامیدن 'to stride/prance,' describing his manner of approach. تفت does not match the provided lexical senses here (likely a scribal/archaic form or exclamation) and is left unspecified.
segments.s704-g51: چوخسرو is چو + خسرو meaning 'when Khosrow' (a temporal subordinate clause head). چنان is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying دید, the past tense (3rd sg.) of دیدن 'to see.' با normally means 'with' and introduces an accompanying notion; اندیان is uncertain in the supplied lexicon and does not match the listed senses here (likely an archaic or corrupted form meaning 'in thought' or similar), so it is left unspecified.
segments.s704-g52: This clause contains a reported speech tag. چین (here functioning like چنان/چنین) would mark 'thus' but the provided senses do not include that use, so it is uncertain. گفت is the verb 'said' (classical past), introducing the quotation; کای is the vocative particle 'O.' نره is an adjective/noun 'male, virile' modifying شیر 'lion.' ژیان follows شیر in a genitive or appositive sense 'of life' (a poetic intensifier). The verb گفت is used as 'said' (verb) though the supplied senses list conversational nouns; that verbal use is intended here.
segments.s704-g53: برین is orthographic fusion of بر + این ('on/against this'), a demonstrative prepositional phrase 'on this' or 'against this (elephant).' پیل is the direct noun 'elephant.' برتیرباران is a compound adverbial/accusative phrase 'under an arrow‑shower' or 'with arrow‑rain' describing the manner/result. کنید is the 2nd person plural imperative/jussive of کردن 'do/make' used here as a command 'do / let (it be done)' and governs the action ('pelt').
segments.s704-g54: کمان را places 'the bow' as definite object with the object marker را (fronted for emphasis). چوابر is a simile formed from چو (like/as) + ابر (cloud) meaning 'like a cloud.' بهاران qualifies the cloud as 'of spring' or 'spring‑clouds,' a poetic image for a dense volley of arrows. کنید repeats the imperative 'do/bring about' and ties the simile to the action (i.e., make your bows/volley like spring clouds).
segments.s704-g55: از ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from/among the Iranians.' آنک is a relative/demonstrative connector ('that / who') linking to the following name. بد is an epithet 'bad/wicked' placed before روزبه, the proper name (a common poetic inversion). Grammatically this introduces Ruzbeh as a member of the Iranian group.
segments.s704-g56: کمان برنهادند is a past tense clause (3rd plural) 'they raised/lifted bows' (برنهادند = بر نهادند). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once' modifying the action. بزه is a noun 'crime/fault' used here as a direct object or result noun in a poetic construction; the clause thus records a collective hostile action framed as blameworthy.
segments.s704-g121: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause 'when/if'. رایت 'standard/banner' is the subject of the clause; چنین است is a predicative phrase 'is thus'. The following words مردان کین form a vocative/object cluster: مردان is an address to the warriors (vocative) and کین is the notion of 'vengeance' linked as the object/theme that will be sought in the next (imperative) segment.
segments.s704-g122: بخواه is an imperative ('seek/ desire'); the imperative verb appears here while its object (کین) was placed before in the previous segment. و links the two coordinated imperatives. مکن is a prohibitive auxiliary 'do not'. تیره روی is a compound where تیره 'dark/gloomy' qualifies روی 'face/countenance', and زمین marks the domain ('the land/earth') — together 'do not make the land's face dark' (do not cast gloom over the land).
segments.s704-g123: بدو is a prepositional/dative phrase 'to him'. گفت here functions as the speech verb 'said' but the provided sense list does not include the verbal sense (see note). خسرو is the speaker's name; the clause that follows (که اینست روی) is reported content introduced by که.
segments.s704-g124: که introduces a subordinate/content clause 'that/which'. گفتی is the reported past-tense 'you said' (the sense list lacks the verbal sense here), and the remainder gives the content of what was said: ز لشکر 'from the army', کنون 'now', یار جوی is an imperative-like verb phrase 'seek a companion' (جوی here functions as 'seek' / 'search for', the content quoted from the addressee).
segments.s704-g125: گزین کرد is the past action 'chose/selected' (verb phrase). In this poetic word order the verb precedes the subject گستهم (Gostaham), which is the agent. ز ایران is a prepositional phrase 'from Iran' specifying origin, and سوار ('riders/horsemen') is the object/what was chosen.
segments.s704-g126: ده و چار is the archaic/poetic way to say 'ten and four' = fourteen (numeral phrase). گردنکش is an adjectival epithet 'neck-breaking' in sense 'headstrong/rebellious' modifying the riders, and نامدار is another adjective 'renowned/famous'.
segments.s704-g127: نخستین is an ordinal 'first'. ازین = از + این ('of these') uses the poetic form ین for 'این'. جنگیان is the plural referring to the fighters/warriors (poetic plural of جنگ), and نام خویش is a possessive phrase 'his own name' composed of نام 'name' + خویش (here written as خوی + ش) meaning 'his'.
segments.s704-g128: A sequence of past verbs coordinated by و: نوشت 'he wrote/inscribed', بیاورد 'he brought/produced', بنهاد 'he placed/laid down' — all agreeing with the same subject (implied 'he'). پیش is a locative/prepositional adverb 'before / in the presence of' indicating where he set the written name.
segments.s704-g89: که here introduces the clause (a relative/complementizer, 'that' / 'who'); هرکو is the archaic fused form of هر که 'whoever'; برادر is the noun predicate 'brother'; بود is the past copula 'was' (predicate marked as past); دوست is a predicative noun 'friend'; به is the dative/prepositional marker 'to/for' used with دوست but the beneficiary/object is left implicit (a common classical Persian economy of expression).
segments.s704-g90: چو functions as a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when' (or 'if' in some contexts) introducing the contrasting case; دشمن بود is a parallel nominal predicate 'was (an) enemy' with بود again as past copula; بی is the negative preposition 'without' that governs the following nouns; پی و پوست literally list two body-part nouns (here 'flesh/inside' and 'skin'); the final به repeats the dative/prepositional particle in parallel to the previous 'دوست به' and links the clause grammatically, an archaic poetic pattern.
segments.s704-g91: تو هم places 'you' as topic with هم 'also/too' for emphasis; this is a verbless descriptive string where دشمن 'enemy', بد تن 'ill-bodied' (بد modifying تن), and ریمنی form a coordinated series of predicates describing 'تو' — a common classical Persian pattern that lists qualities without an explicit copula.
segments.s704-g92: جهان‌آفرین را: را is the definite/object marker marking 'the World‑Creator' as the target of the following psychological attitude; به دل is a locative phrase 'in (the) heart' showing where the attitude resides; دشمنی is the noun 'enmity' — the clause lacks an explicit verb like 'to have' (a common omission in classical Persian), so the meaning is 'you bear/nurse enmity against the Creator in your heart.'
segments.s704-g93: به پیش = 'in the presence of / before'; the doubled برادر برادر is a terse classical way to express 'brother against brother' (first برادر = location/topic 'before brothers/kin', second = the opposing party), and به جنگ 'to war' places the action 'to fight/into battle' after the participants — classical Persian frequently orders prepositional phrases and noun phrases before the verb.
segments.s704-g94: نیاید is a negative modal/predicative 'should not (occur)/ought not to come'; اگر introduces the condition 'if'; باشدش is the fused form of باشد + ش (او) meaning 'it be to him' / 'he has' (i.e. 'if he possesses'); نام و ننگ is a fixed pair 'name and shame' (here 'reputation and sense of shame' — i.e. honour), so the clause reads 'this should not happen if he has any fame and shame' = 'if he cares for his honour.'
segments.s704-g95: چو combined with شنید yields the temporal clause 'چو شنید' contracted here as چوبشنید 'when he heard'; بهرام is the subject; ز (poetic for از) means 'from' or 'of' and z + او (here elided as z(o) = 'from him'); بازگشت is the verb 'returned' (here a past action).
segments.s704-g96: برآشفت is a past intransitive verb 'he became angry/was incensed'; و links actions; با او 'toward/with him' marks the target of the following state; دژم ساز گشت is a light‑verb construction where ساز (literally 'make') plus گشت 'became' yields 'became/grow sullen' — literally 'made sullen' → 'turned grim.'
segments.s704-g217: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (gaze) + کرد (did/made) = 'looked' (perfective past). The subject خسرو follows the verb (inverted order common in poetry). پس پشت خویش = 'behind his back / over his shoulder'; پس 'after/behind' + پشت 'back/rear' + خویش split here into خوی + ش (possessive), yielding 'his own'. Word order is somewhat fluid for poetic effect.
segments.s704-g218: ازان چار بهرام را دید پیش: دید is the main past tense verb 'saw'. را is the definite/direct-object marker attached to بهرام. ازان چار here means 'of those four' (ازان = 'of those / from those'; چار = 'four'), and پیش at the end means 'before/in front of' — so the clause says he 'saw Bahram before/in front of those four'.
segments.s704-g219: همی‌داشت is an imperfective/past habitual auxiliary meaning 'was keeping' or 'kept (continuously)'. تن را is the definite object 'the body' (تن + را). رازدشمن is a genitive compound (رازِ دشمن) 'the enemy's secret' or 'of the enemy' modifying or linked to تن; نگاه ('gaze') at the end names what he kept — 'he kept his gaze on the enemy's body/secret' (word order here places the object and its modifier before نگاه).
segments.s704-g220: ببرید is a past action-form (here 'cut off' / 'was cut'), followed by برگستوان which refers to the shoulder/arm region (بر + گستوان) — the phrase means 'cut off at the shoulder'. سیاه 'black' qualifies the person/limb. The clause is succinct/elliptical as is typical in epic verse: subject may be omitted and the event stated tersely.
segments.s704-g221: ازو بازماندند is a compound verb phrase: ازو ('from him' / 'from that') + بازماندند (they remained/were left behind). هردوسوار is the subject 'both riders'. The verb is plural (‑ند) agreeing with هردوسوار.
segments.s704-g222: پس پشت here reiterates 'behind' (پس = after/then, پشت = rear/behind) and modifies the following noun phrase. اودشمن is 'that enemy' (او + دشمن used as a determiner phrase), and کینه‌دار is a compound adjective 'bearing grudge' / 'vengeful' (کینه 'hatred' + دار 'having/possessing') describing the enemy. The phrase as a whole places the riders behind that resentful foe.
segments.s704-g223: به پیش اندر آمد is a locative-motion phrase 'came forward / came to the front' (به پیش = toward the front; اندر آمد = entered/came in). یکی غار تنگ follows: یکی 'a certain one' + غار تنگ 'narrow cave', so the line implies 'one (person) came out/in from a narrow cave' — poetic omission of an explicit preposition 'from' is common.
segments.s704-g224: سه جنگی is an epithet: سه (three) + جنگی (warlike/fighter) — a stock heroic compound meaning a seasoned/three‑fought warrior. پس اندر here again gives a temporal/locative 'then/within' and بسان پلنگ 'like a leopard' is a simile (بسان = like, پلنگ = leopard). The whole segment describes his fierce, pouncing manner.
segments.s704-g25: بفرمود is a past, honorific/third‑person verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. Here it introduces a subordinate clause (تاکوس...) with تا functioning as a subordinating particle; برپشت پیل is a locative phrase 'on the back of the elephant' (برپشت = 'upon the back of', followed by the noun پیل = 'elephant'). Word order is Verb + (subordinate marker + object/place): 'he ordered that [the drum] on the elephant's back...'.
segments.s704-g26: ببستند is a past plural verb 'they bound/tied' (the action following the order). وشد is the fused conjunction + verb 'and became/was formed' (و + شد). گرد لشکر functions as a circumstantial expression meaning 'a ring/circle about the army' (گرد = 'around/about', لشکر = 'host/army'). چونیل is a simile/comparison element ('like/as'), completing the image. The clause describes the result of the fastening: they tied it and a ring formed around the host.
segments.s704-g27: نشست is a past verb 'he sat/settled' used here as 'he alighted/stepped down'. ازبرپشت is a fused locative phrase 'from off the back' (از + بر + پشت) meaning 'from the (top of the) back of', and پیل سپید is the noun + adjective 'the white elephant' (سپید follows the noun as an attributive adjective). The whole segment: 'he alighted from the back of the white elephant.'
segments.s704-g28: هم here is an adverb 'also/then' or a linking particle introducing the next clause. آوردش is a transitive past verb 'brought him/it' with the object clitic -ش referring back to the person; ازبخت is an adverbial 'by fate' or 'by fortune' marking cause; شد is the past copula/verb 'became'; ناامید is the predicate adjective 'hopeless/despairing'. Together: 'by fate he was brought (so) and became hopeless.'
segments.s704-g29: همی‌راند is an imperfective/present‑habitual form 'he was driving / he drove continuously' (همی‑ marks ongoing/habitual action). آن پیل is the demonstrative + noun 'that elephant' as subject. تامیمنه appears as an epithet or adverbial qualifier attached to پیل (its exact meaning is archaic/uncertain here and functions as a descriptive tag), so its sense is contextually idiomatic rather than one of the modern dictionary senses given.
segments.s704-g30: بشاپور گفت is 'he said to Shapur' — here the prepositional particle به is fused to شاپور (بشاپور) meaning 'to Shapur', followed by the verb گفت 'said'. ای introduces a vocative 'O!', and بد بدتنه is a duplicate intensifying adjective phrase 'bad, ill‑bodied / ill‑favored one' addressed to someone. Word order is indirect object ('to Shapur') + verb + direct speech/vocative.
segments.s704-g31: نه is a negation 'no/not'. پیمانت is the noun پیمان 'pledge/covenant' with the second‑person enclitic -ت 'your' (پیمانت = 'your covenant'). این بد is 'this bad/this sort' (این = 'this', بد = 'bad/ill/unsuitable') qualifying the pact, and به نامه درون is a locative/genitive phrase 'in the written letter/record' (به نامه = 'in/into the letter', درون = 'within'). The segment denies that the person's pledge is of that character in the document.
segments.s704-g32: که introduces a content clause 'that/which'. پیش من آیی is 'you come before me / appear in my presence' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', من = 'me', آیی = present/subjunctive 'you come'). بدین دشت خون is a locative phrase 'to this blood‑stained plain' where بدین = ب + این 'to this' and دشت خون = 'plain of blood' (خون qualifying دشت). Overall: 'that you should come before me into this bloody plain.'
segments.s704-g81: دوخونی (compound) names the parties as ‘of two bloods’ or ‘mixed-bloods’ and functions as the subject. بران سان is a fused adverbial phrase (بر + آن + سان) meaning ‘thereupon / in that manner’ placed before the verb for emphasis. برآویختند is past tense, 3rd-person plural (they sprang upon/attacked); the prefix بر- gives a directional or on‑to sense.
segments.s704-g82: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ('so that' / 'which'), followed by گفتی used elliptically (literally 'speech' / 'as has been said' in this poetic usage). بهمشان = به + هم + ـشان means 'to one another / among them'. برآمیختند is past 3pl 'they mixed / mingled', completing the result sense started by که.
segments.s704-g83: بدین سان is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'in this way / thus' (here split orthographically). زمانی is a temporal noun 'a while / a time' and برآمد is a past verb 'arose / occurred' — together 'a long time came about / passed'. دراز is an adjective 'long' modifying زمانی; the overall clause means 'a long time passed/ensued.'
segments.s704-g84: همی is a classical/poetic particle indicating continuous aspect ('still / for a time'). یک زدیگر is an older variant of یکدیگر ('one another'). نگشتند is past negative (ن + گشتند) meaning 'they did not turn / return'. باز at the end is the adverb 'again' — together: 'they did not turn to one another again.'
segments.s704-g85: بدو is the short prepositional form of به + او ('to him'). گفت is the past tense 'said' used before the subject (verb–subject order is common in poetry): 'to him said Bahram'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' attached to the name; بی‌پدر is an insulting vocative/adjective 'fatherless / bastard' addressing the addressee.
segments.s704-g86: به خون برادر is an instrumental/oblique phrase 'with / by (the) brother's blood' (به marks relation/instrument). چه is the interrogative/exclamatory 'what / why' here introducing a rhetorical question. بندی is 2nd-person singular verb form 'you bind / you gird' (present/simple), and کمر is 'waist / belt' — literally 'why do you gird your waist with your brother's blood?'.
segments.s704-g87: بدو گفت again 'he said to him' (به + او). گردوی here is an epithet/nickname literally 'walnut-like / walnut-colored' used as an insulting epithet preceding the vocative. کای is the vocative 'O'. پیسه گرگ is a compound insult: پیسه 'pittance / small coin' modifying گرگ 'wolf' figuratively ('miserly / predatory one'). The sequence are vocative epithets prior to the reported speech.
segments.s704-g88: تونشنیدی is the contracted colloquial/poetic form تو نشنیدی 'have you not heard?' (2nd person question with negative nuance). آن داستان بزرگ is the object phrase 'that great story/tale'. The whole segment is a rhetorical question: 'Haven't you heard that great tale?'.
segments.s704-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. ایرانیان is the subject (third-person plural). برکشیدند is a past-tense, third-person-plural verb ('they raised/drew up') used here in a literary/poetic register; the object صف follows the verb (poetic inversion) and means the battle line/formation that they 'raised' or 'drew up'.
segments.s704-g10: همه ('everyone' / 'all') quantifies the following noun phrase. نیزه و تیغ هندی is a coordinated noun phrase 'spears and Indian swords' (هندی is a postpositive adjective/epithet modifying تیغ). بکف is the fused poetic form of به‌کف / به کف meaning 'in (their) hand(s)' and serves as a locative/adverbial finishing the clause.
segments.s704-g11: زمین سر به سر is an idiomatic locative phrase 'the earth, all over' (سر به سر = 'throughout/entirely'). گفتی is a literary use here (related to 'it is said' / 'as one says' — a frozen/formal usage in poetry), and ازجوشنست is a fused form of از جوشن است ('is of/like armor'), yielding the sense 'the earth everywhere seemed as if made of armor'.
segments.s704-g12: ستاره is the subject 'the star' (here used poetically for stellar lights). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (short form of از). نوک سنان is a genitive construction 'the tips of spears' (نوک + سنان). روشنست is a stative copula 'is bright / shines' (poetic present). The clause says the stars gleam from spear-tips.
segments.s704-g13: چو is the temporal 'when'. خسرو is the proper-name subject. بیاراست is a literary past verb 'arrayed/arrayed himself' (بیاراستن = to adorn/array). بر قلبگاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon his breast/heart' (بر + قلبگاه) giving the place where the action occurs; word order is poetic with the locative following the verb.
segments.s704-g14: همه دل گرفتند is a compact expression: همه modifies دل ('all hearts'), and گرفتند (past 3pl) here carries the middle/affected sense 'were seized / were moved' (not a simple transitive 'they seized'). یکسر سپاه is an intensifying phrase 'the whole/entire army' (یکسر = entirely/at once + سپاه = army) that specifies whose hearts are meant — the entire host.
segments.s704-g15: ورامیمنه is a fused locative phrase (vora + mīmanah / میمنه) used in poetry to mean 'on his right (hand / side)'. دار here is used nominally (in older/poetic usage) together with گردوی to indicate the person or thing characterized by walnut (گردوی) — literally 'a walnut one' / 'one of walnut hue' — and بود is the past copula 'was'. Overall this segment establishes that on his right stood/was a walnut‑(coloured) figure or object (the precise referent is condensed in the poetic diction).
segments.s704-g16: که is the relative 'who/which/that', linking back to the person/thing just mentioned. گرد here functions as an adjective 'round' or 'rounded' (poetic use of گرد meaning 'round/rounded'); ودلیر and وجهانجوی are fused conjunctive forms (و + دلیر = 'and brave', و + جهانجوی = 'and world‑seeking/ambitious'). بود is the past copula 'was'. The series gives three attributive adjectives describing that right‑side figure.
segments.s704-g169: برین is the compound 'بر + این' (literally 'for/at this') marking the cause or circumstance. مایه is a noun 'cause/resource' and مردم is the subject 'the people'. به جنگ آمدست is a poetic past/perfect construction: آمدست = 'has come' (archaic/poetic 3rd‑person form) and به جنگ = 'to/into battle' (prepositional phrase). The overall order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (circumstantial phrase first, then subject, then verbal idea).
segments.s704-g170: وگر is the conditional 'if/otherwise'. پیش works adverbially here as 'before/in front of' and combines with کام 'desire/wish' (پیش کام = 'before its desire' / 'prior to fulfilling its wish'). نهنگ is the subject 'the whale' (used literally or metaphorically) and آمدست is the same archaic perfect 'has come'. The clause gives an alternative or contrast introduced by وگر.
segments.s704-g171: فزون نیست is a negated predication meaning 'is not more' / 'there are not more'. با او is the prepositional phrase 'with him'. سرافراز is an epithet 'lofty/triumphant/proud' qualifying the following numeral phrase; the clause as a whole states there are at most twenty such men with him. Note the poetically flexible order (adjective + numeral).
segments.s704-g172: ازیشان (از + ایشان) means 'of/from them' (i.e. 'among them'). کسی را is the indefinite object 'anyone' with را marking the definite/defocused object. ندانم is the 1st‑person negative present 'I do not know' (archaic shape instead of modern نمی‌دانم). که introduces the free relative 'who', and کیست is 'who is' (کی + ست). Together: 'Of them I know no one who (I can say) who he is.'
segments.s704-g173: This is a conditional protasis: اگر 'if' + پیشم 'before me / in my presence' (adverbial) + آید 'comes' (3rd‑person present/future). جهان را is the definite object 'the world' marked by را, and بسم (بس + ـم) is a predicative 'it is enough / it suffices'. The clause means 'if the world comes to me, it will be enough.'
segments.s704-g174: Another conditional: اگر 'if' + بر نیایم where بر here has the directional/against/to sense and نیایم is the negative 1st‑person form 'I do not come/attain' (poetic/archaic shape). ازو 'from/of it/him' is the source, and ناکسم is a predicative first‑person form 'I am a wretch' (ناکِس + ـم). The clause says 'if I do not obtain it, I am wretched.'
segments.s704-g175: This clause places the dative prepositional phrase به ایزد 'to/unto God' first, then the subject گشسپ (Goshasp). ویلان سینه is an ablative/qualifying phrase 'with a forlorn/distressed breast' (ویلان modifying سینه), describing Goshasp's emotional state. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (poetic diction), so the structure is 'To God, Goshasp, with a distressed chest, said...'.
segments.s704-g176: که introduces the content clause 'that'. مردان is the subject 'the men' (or 'heroes'), and ندارند is the present plural 'they do not have'. مردی is the indefinite object 'a man' and نهفت is an adjective/participle 'hidden/concealed' following the noun it qualifies. The clause literally says 'that the men have no hidden man.'
segments.s704-g201: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' (poetic). بهرام is the proper name (subject). جنگی is an adjectival use meaning 'warlike' modifying اسپ, so 'جنگی اسپ' = 'war-steed'. برانگیخت is a past tense (3rd person singular) literary verb 'roused/spurred' taking اسپ as its object. Word order is inverted/condensed in poetic style (conjunction + subject + adjective + verb + object).
segments.s704-g202: یلان is plural 'heroes/warriors' (subject/agent group). سینه is literal 'chest/breast' used metonymically for ardor or heaving chests. و is the coordinating 'and'. گرد here functions as a noun/adverb meaning 'around/about' or 'the throng about' and links to the following title; ایزد (literary epithet 'divine') modifies گشسپ (Goshasp) without an explicit ezafe — a poetic compression meaning 'the company about the divine Goshasp'. The segment describes the assembled warriors and Goshasp's retinue reacting together.
segments.s704-g203: بدیدند is the past tense 3rd-person plural 'they saw'. The sequence places the verb before its subject for poetic effect; یاران 'companions' with خسروهمه (Khosrow + همه) yields 'all the companions of Khosrow' (i.e. all Khosrow's followers). The phrase means 'all of Khosrow's companions saw'.
segments.s704-g204: شد is the past 3rd-singular 'became' (copula/verb of change). او 'he' is the subject; گرگ is the predicate noun 'a wolf' (metaphorical). The conjunction و links a coordinate clause whose ellipted verb is recovered from شد: آن 'those/the', نامداران 'renowned ones' (famous warriors), رمه 'flock/herd' — i.e. 'he became a wolf, and those renowned men became a flock.' The verb is only stated once and applies to both coordinated predicates, a common poetic ellipsis.
segments.s704-g205: بماند is a past / perfect-like form 'remained' or 'held back'. آنگهی is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic form of از) and introduces the verbal-noun phrase آویختن (infinitive/masdar 'to cling/attach oneself' — here idiomatically 'to fall upon' or 'to join the fight'). The construction means the king stayed back / refrained from plunging in (i.e. he 'remained' from the action expressed by the infinitive).
segments.s704-g206: وزان is a compressed compound of و + ز آن 'and from that' (archaic/poetic connective). شورش is 'uprising/tumult' (here 'uproar/commotion' caused by the action). و is 'and'. باره is a noun meaning 'occasion/instance' or 'another onset' and combines with انگیختن (infinitive 'to rouse/raise') — together meaning 'to stir up an uproar/raise another attack'. Grammatically the infinitive انگیختن names the action caused by the preceding phrase; the subject is understood from the previous clause (elliptical linkage typical in poetry).
segments.s704-g207: جهاندار is an honorific title 'sovereign' (literally 'world-holder'); ناکام is the adjective 'unsuccessful/thwarted' modifying it. برگاشت is a past 3rd-singular verb 'he took up / took back / remounted' — here idiomatically 'remounted' or 'took back his steed'. اسپ is the object 'steed/horse'. The adjective follows the title in typical epic compression: 'the sovereign, defeated, remounted his steed.'
segments.s704-g208: پس is an adverb 'then/thereupon'. اندر is the locative/prepositional 'into/among'. همی‌رفت is an archaic imperfect/progressive form with the preverb همی- meaning 'was going' / 'proceeded' (classical narrative tense). ایزدگشسپ is a compounded epic name/title 'Izad‑Goshasp' (the divine Goshasp) and is the subject that follows the verb by poetic inversion: 'then Izad‑Goshasp went in among them.'
segments.s704-g209: چو here is the subordinating conjunction 'when' placed before a list of proper names (here written fused to the first name). The following personal names are coordinated with و (and); ماند is a simple past/present-stative verb meaning 'remained/stood' (3rd singular) giving the time-frame 'when X, Y and Z stood/ remained.'
segments.s704-g210: The sequence "X نام یزدان بخواند" means 'X called/ invoked the name of God.' نام is the direct object 'name' and یزدان is 'God' (object of the name). بخواند is an older/past-form verb 'he called/invoked' (3rd singular past/ perfect sense in epic diction).
segments.s704-g211: بدو + имя + گفت here functions as 'the king said to Gostaham' with بدو = 'to him/to Gostaham' (prepositional recipient) and گفت = 'said'. آن زمان 'then/at that time' specifies when; شهریار is the addressee/title 'the king'. Word order reflects a typical indirect speech frame (recipient + said + temporal phrase + vocative/title).
segments.s704-g212: که introduces the reported clause ('that ...'). تنگ اندرآمد is a compound idiom: تنگ = 'tight/narrow' and اندرآمد literally 'entered/came in', together meaning 'grew tight/pressure/close' (i.e., trouble closed in). بد modifies روزگار 'bad times/fate' — روزگار here = 'times' or 'fortune'. The whole clause means 'that bad times had come upon them.'
segments.s704-g213: This is a rhetorical question: چه بایست = 'what need is there / why should (this happen)'; این بیهده رستخیز = 'this pointless/useless uprising' (این = 'this', بیهده = 'futile/pointless', رستخیز = 'uprising/resurrection' used here as 'uprising/assault'). The verb بایست expresses necessity/oughtness in an exclamatory sense.
segments.s704-g214: بدیدند (they saw) + پشت من (my back) + اندر گریز (in flight) is a compact phrase meaning 'they saw my back as I fled.' اندر here marks the circumstance 'in/while (in)'; گریز = 'flight/escape.' The verb is perfective past 'they saw.'
segments.s704-g215: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'); the following token گستهم here is vocative 'Gostaham' (name addressed). کامد سوار is an inverted clause 'a rider has come/arrived' where کامد is an archaic/epic present/past form 'comes/has come' and سوار = 'rider/horseman.'
segments.s704-g216: توتنهاشدی is the fused colloquial/poetic form تو تنها شدی = 'you have become alone' (2nd person past/ perfect). چون here is interrogative 'how', and کنی is the present/subjunctive 2nd-person form of 'to do/make' used to ask 'how will you do / how will you act'; کارزار = 'battle/field.' The sequence asks 'you are alone — how will you wage the battle?'
segments.s704-g281: چوآمد is the fused archaic conjunction + verb (چو + آمد) meaning “when he came.” به مریم is a prepositional phrase “to Mary” (indirect object). بگفت is the simple past “he said / told” (ب‑ prefix + گفت). آنچ دید is an older form of آن‌چه دید “that which he saw” (آنچ = آن‌چه; دید is past ‘saw’), so together بگفت آنچ دید = “he told (her) what he had seen.”
segments.s704-g282: وزان = و + آن = “and from that.” کوه خارا is a noun + adjective “flinty/hard mountain” (کوه ‘mountain’, خارا ‘flinty, hard’). سر اندر کشید is a verbal phrase where سر ‘head’ + اندر ‘into/in’ + کشید (past of کشیدن ‘to draw/pull’) => “drew his head in/into (it),” i.e. withdrew into the mountain.
segments.s704-g283: چنین گفت = “thus he said” (چنین = thus; گفت here is the past verb ‘said’). کای is a vocative “O” (used to address someone). ماه here is a poetic vocative “moon” / “beautiful one” used as a compliment. قیصر نژاد is a compound ‘of Caesar’s lineage’ (scion of Caesar) forming the addressee: “O moon, scion of Caesar.”
segments.s704-g284: مرا is the accusative ‘me’ (split in the edition as م + را). داور دادگر is a noun phrase ‘a judge, just (judge)’ (داور ‘judge’ + دادگر ‘just’). The doubled phrase داد داد is an older poetic sequence: the first داد can be read as the noun ‘justice’ and the final داد as the past verb ‘gave’ (i.e. “justice he gave”), producing the sense “give me a just judge — (grant) justice” (or more loosely “give me a just judge who will do justice”).
segments.s704-g285: The phrase sets up a contrast using نه ... از ...: نه (not) negates the first stated cause. از marks the source/cause (‘from’), so ازِ کاهلی = ‘from laziness’. The following ازِ بد دِلی gives the contrasted cause (‘but from ill‑heartedness’). The medial form بدنه in the manuscript is an archaic/poetic fusion that links the negation to the contrast (functionally reading as part of the negation/contrast rather than a standalone noun).
segments.s704-g286: که introduces an explanatory subordinate clause here (≈ ‘for’/‘because’ or ‘that’). در جنگ = ‘in battle’ (در = in, جنگ = battle). بد دل کند is a causal clause: دلِ بد ‘a bad heart’ as subject and کند (3rd‑person singular of کردن used causatively) = ‘makes/causes’. کاهلی is the effect ‘negligence/laziness’. Together: ‘for in battle a bad heart causes negligence.’
