segments.s704-g287: بدان here is a compound directional/demonstrative (‘to that’) built from بـ (to/into) + دان (an older form corresponding to ‘that’); it points to the specific cave. غار بی‌راه = ‘a cave off the road’ or ‘a cave out of the way’ (بی‌راه modifying غار). در ماندم: در = ‘inside/in’, ماندم = first‑person past of ماندن ‘I remained/was left’. Word order places the locative در before the verb ماندم.
segments.s704-g288: به دل is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘in (my) heart’ or ‘inwardly’ (به = to/into, دل = heart/mind). آفریننده را is the definite object: آفریننده ‘the Creator’ and را marks it as the direct object. خواندم is first‑person past of خواندن and here means ‘I called upon/invoked’ (not merely ‘read’): the subject (I) is understood and the verb reports the act of calling/praying.
segments.s704-g161: This is a compact nominal phrase plus verb: جهان جوی (a compound: 'world' + 'seeker') is the subject, بیدار دل is a adjectival phrase 'wakeful/alert of heart' placed after the noun (poetic word order), and برنشست is the simple past (archaic/poetic) verb meaning 'sat up / took a posture.' The order (subject + attribute + past verb) is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated diction.
segments.s704-g162: This is a compact circumstantial phrase describing what he took/ wore: کمندی 'a lasso/snare' marked by the preposition به + فتراک 'to/at the saddle-cloth' (indicating where the lasso was hung), then و joins the next image, تیغی بدست is a condensed idiom 'a sword in (his) hand' (تیغ + indefinite/partitive -ی + بدست 'in hand'). The verbs are omitted — these are participial/nominal supplements describing his equipment/state.
segments.s704-g163: ز بالا is an adverbial phrase 'from above / from on high.' چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' and introduces the subordinate clause; آن مایه مردم is the object 'that mass/contingent of people' (مایه used as 'force, mass'), and بدید is the simple past 'he saw.' The clause order (adverbial + چو + object + past verb) is idiomatic poetic syntax.
segments.s704-g164: تنی چند is an idiom meaning 'a few / several (persons)' with تنی used in the sense 'some, a certain (person).' زان 'of/from that' links back to آن (that contingent), جنگیان is the plural 'warriors/fighters,' and برگزید is the past verb 'he selected/chose.' The whole is a short result clause ('he selected a few of those warriors').
segments.s704-g165: یلان (yelān) is the plural subject 'the heroes/champions.' سینه را گفت is a poetic idiom: سینه را (literally 'their chest' with the object marker را) + گفت 'said/cried' — meaning 'they cried out from their chests' or 'they declared aloud.' کاین begins a reported clause 'that this...' and بد نژاد is a compound adjective 'ill-born / of bad lineage' (بد modifying نژاد).
segments.s704-g166: This completes the reported content. به جنگ اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto the battle' or 'into the fighting.' دادمردی here is most naturally read as a proper name (Dadmardi) rather than the abstract noun 'chivalry' (the lexicon allows both), and بداد is the past verb form 'gave/sent' (archaic poetic past) — so together the clause says 'he sent Dadmardi into the battle.' The sequence is object/recipient then verb, typical in poetic compression.
segments.s704-g167: که introduces an explanation 'that.' من دانم is the first-person present 'I know,' کنون 'now' is an adverb, and جزو نیست این is an inverted predication: جزو 'a part/member' + نیست 'is not' + این 'this' (inverted order for poetic effect) — 'I know now that this is not a member/part (i.e. not one of them).'
segments.s704-g168: This relative clause specifies what kind of person he is not: که introduces 'that,' یارد 'an ally/companion' is the role, چمیدن is the infinitive 'to spring up / to leap forth' used here elliptically (i.e. 'one whom a companion would spring to'), برین is the fused form بر + این 'on this,' دشت 'plain,' and کین 'of vengeance' — altogether: 'one to whom an ally would spring up on this plain of vengeance' (negated by the previous clause).
segments.s704-g177: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal clause meaning 'it should not be / we should not'. The verb phrase ما ... باشیم uses the present-subjunctive form باشیم ('that we be') after that-clause; بیش modifies باشیم as 'more' (i.e. 'be more/greater in number'). چار follows as the predicative numeral 'four' — archaic word order puts the numeral after the predicate.
segments.s704-g178: به خسرو is a prepositional phrase 'to/for Khosrow' placed before the clause for emphasis. مرا is split here into م + را: م is the enclitic accusative 'me' and را is the definite/object marker; کس is the indefinite subject 'anyone/someone'. نیاید is an archaic negative-present/optative-form meaning 'does not/shall not come' i.e. 'does not come to help', and به کار is a prepositional idiom 'to (any) use' = 'of use/aid'. Overall: 'for Khosrow no one comes to my use' → 'no one is of use to me against Khosrow'.
segments.s704-g179: یکی بد is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain bad one / an evil fellow'. The clause is elliptical: the copula is omitted and کجا functions here as an exclamatory/interrogative marker introducing the following identification (poetic usage). نام او 'his name' is a straightforward possessive noun phrase, and جان فروز is a compound proper name literally meaning 'life‑illuminating' used here as the man's name.
segments.s704-g180: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'who/that'. تیره شبان is a nominal phrase 'dark/gloomy shepherds' (تیره modifying شبان). برگزیدی is the simple past 2nd‑person 'you chose' (برگزیدن). به‌روز is an adverbial phrase 'for the day / in daylight' (literally 'to the day'), specifying when/for what purpose they were chosen.
segments.s704-g181: سپه را places the definite direct object before the verb with the object marker را ('the army'). بدو is the clitic prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him'. داد is the past verb 'gave'. The conjunction و links to خود پیش رفت where خود is an emphatic/reflexive 'he himself' and پیش رفت is 'went forward' (پیش as adverb 'ahead' + رفت past 'went').
segments.s704-g182: همی is an archaic/progressive particle often used with past forms to signal continuous or emphatic action ('was, still'). تاخت is the past 'galloped/rushed/charged'. با این سه is a prepositional phrase 'with these three' (این سه = 'these three'), بیدار functions as an attributive adjective 'alert/awake'. تفت here is poetic/epithetic — literally 'scorching' or 'burning' — probably used as a fierce epithet; its literal lexical senses are verbal but it is functioning adjectivally or as a name/epithet in context.
segments.s704-g183: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بهرام را is the definite object (Bahrām) marked by را and placed before the verb for emphasis. دید is the past tense 'saw' (he beheld). خسرو is the subject (Khosrow) and ز راه is an ablative/locative phrase 'from the road / along the road' describing Bahram's coming.
segments.s704-g184: به ایرانیان is an indirect object 'to the Iranians' (dative). گفت is the past 'said' (here introducing reported speech). کامد is an archaic third‑person present/future 'comes / is coming', and سپاه is the noun 'army' — together reporting Khosrow's words 'the army is coming'.
segments.s704-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بر زد is a poetic past verb complex meaning 'rose/was raised' (بر as directional/prefix + زد 'struck/raised'); ز دریا is a prepositional phrase 'from the sea' (ز = from, دریا = sea). درفش سپید is the noun phrase 'the white standard/banner' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s704-g2: ستاره is the subject 'the star'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' used as a copula meaning 'became'. از تیرگی marks cause ('because of/from the darkness') with از introducing the reason, and ناامید is the resulting predicate adjective 'hopeless'. Word order follows noun–verb–prepositional phrase–adjective.
segments.s704-g3: تبیره زنان is a nominal phrase: تبیره modifies/qualifies زنان (here 'مournful/tormenting/lamenting women' in context), and از دو پرده سرای is a source phrase 'from the two curtains of the chamber' (از + numeral + پرده + سرای). In poetic register modifiers and nouns can invert their normal order.
segments.s704-g4: برفتند is the past plural 'they went away/advanced' (بر- prefix giving a sense of forth/up + رفتند 'they went'). با introduces accompaniment 'with', repeated implicitly before باکرنای (here written as one word) so the phrase lists companions/tools: پیل 'elephant' and باکرنای 'with trumpets' (instrumental accompaniment).
segments.s704-g5: خروش آمد is a simple nominal clause: خروش 'a roar' + آمد 'came/arose'. و links the next nominal phrase ناله گاودم, where ناله is 'moan/cry' and گاودم (literally 'cow-tail') modifies or characterizes that moan (a figurative description).
segments.s704-g6: هم is the adverb 'also/likewise' linking to the preceding clause. از introduces the source 'from', and کوهه پیل is a genitive/construct 'the mountain of the elephant' (کوهه = mountain, پیل = elephant). رویینه خم is a descriptive compound after پیل: رویینه 'armored/iron-clad' + خم 'bend/hump', describing the elephant's armored hump; modifiers follow the noun in this poetic phrasing.
segments.s704-g7: تو is the 2nd‑person singular subject 'you'. گفتی here functions as 'you said' (the listed dictionary senses are nominal but the line reads as the verb 'گفتی'); بجنبد is a present/subjunctive form 'would move/would stir' (ب- prefix plus جنبیدن), همی is a continuous/intonational particle often giving iterative or emphatic sense, and دشت is the direct object 'plain'. وراغ is an uncertain/poetic adjective or adverb tied to دشت; the whole clause reports what 'you said'.
segments.s704-g8: شده is the perfect/past participle 'has become'. روی خورشید is a genitive phrase 'the face (or surface) of the sun' (روی = face/surface + خورشید = sun). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and پر زاغ is the simile's vehicle 'a crow's feather' (پر = feather, زاغ = crow) — together: 'has become like a crow's feather' (i.e. darkened).
segments.s704-g241: بدین جای بیچارگی is a short nominal phrase meaning “in this place/state of misery.” بدین = به + این (classical fused form) gives the adverbial sense “in/at this,” جای is the noun “place/state,” and بیچارگی is the abstract noun “misery/helplessness.” As a whole it functions as the circumstance for the plea that follows.
segments.s704-g242: تو باشی is an optative/imperative-like form: باشی addresses the addressee with a wish/imperative sense (“may you be” / “be”). ننالم is the speaker’s verb ‘I do not lament/complain’ (negative present/optative flavor in classical diction). به introduces the indirect object (the addressee of the complaining) and کیوان و تیر are the coordinated objects (“Kiwan and Tir,” here celestial/fateful powers). The clause therefore says: if you are my helper, I will not complain to those powers.
segments.s704-g243: هم آنگه چو ... برشد is a temporal clause: هم آنگه = “and then,” چو = “when,” از کوه is the source adjunct “from the mountain,” and برشد is a past third-person verb “rose/was raised.” خروش is the subject (the roar). Word order is typical poetic: temporal marker + subordinating particle + source phrase + verb + subject.
segments.s704-g244: پدید آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning “appeared” (پدید = manifest/visible + آمد = came). از راه gives the direction/instrumental ‘from the way/on the road,’ and فرخ سروش is the noun phrase ‘the auspicious/fortunate Soroush’ (Soroush = divine messenger). The verb construction here places the verb phrase before the noun phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s704-g245: همه جامه‌اش = “all his garment(s)” (همه modifies جامه‌اش). سبز و خنگی are two adjectives joined by و describing those garments; the phrase به زیر follows as an adverbial (‘beneath’ / ‘hanging down’). This is a descriptive, verbless clause characterizing the messenger’s appearance. (Note: خنگی is an archaic/ambiguous word here; its exact meaning in this passage is debated in editions.)
segments.s704-g246: ز دیدار او = “from/because of the sight of him” (ز = ‘from/because of’ introducing the causal phrase). گشت is the past of گشتن used here in the sense “became,” and خسرو دلیر is the nominative + predicate: ‘Khosrow (became) brave.’ The clause thus gives the effect: on seeing that figure, Khosrow grew bold.
segments.s704-g247: چو نزدیک شد is a temporal clause ‘when he drew near/approached’ (چو = when, نزدیک شد = came near). دست خسرو گرفت is the main clause: گرفت is the past verb ‘(he) took/seized’ and دست خسرو is its object ‘Khosrow’s hand.’ The sequence is temporal clause → main action, typical narrative order.
segments.s704-g248: ز یزدان پاک = ‘from/because of the pure God’ (z = from/because of; یزدان پاک = the pure/divine God). این نباشد شگفت is an idiomatic classical statement: نباشد شگفت = ‘it is not a wonder’ (here functioning as a plain negative copula + predicate); thus ‘coming from God, this is not surprising.’ Note the slightly archaic use of نباشد as an assertive negative copula in classical poetry.
segments.s704-g105: Word order is topical: the prepositional phrase 'به گستهم' (to Gostaham) comes before the verb 'گفت' (said), followed by the time phrase 'آن زمان' and the subject 'شهریار' (the king). 'گفت' is a past-tense verb meaning 'said' (here introducing reported speech).
segments.s704-g106: This is a conditional clause introduced by the archaic compound 'که‌گر' = 'if' (or 'if ever'). 'هیچ' here functions indefinitively ('any/ever'); 'رومی' names the enemy (the Rûm/Romans/Byzantines); 'کند' is the classical 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of 'کردن' used to mean 'does/makes' (here 'wages'); 'کارزار' = 'war, battle'. Together they form 'if any Roman wages war...'.
segments.s704-g107: 'چو' here is the conditional/temporal particle 'if/when'. 'بهرام' is the proper name. 'جنگی' is an epithet/adjective tied to Bahram meaning 'warlike' or 'in battle' (classical usage can mean 'the warrior/Bahram in war'). 'شکسته شود' is a passive/result clause: 'شکسته' (broken/defeated) plus 'شود' (present/subjunctive of 'شدن') giving 'is broken/defeated'.
segments.s704-g108: 'وگر' = 'but if' / 'and if' provides an alternative conditional; 'نیز' = 'also'. 'در' is the preposition 'in'; 'جنگ' = 'battle/war'; 'خسته شود' again uses the adjective 'خسته' (weary) with 'شود' to mean 'becomes weary' or 'grow tired' (present/subjunctive/passive sense).
segments.s704-g109: 'همه رومیان' is the plural subject 'all the Romans'. The phrase 'سر به گردون برند' is an idiomatic/clausal sequence: 'سر به گردون' = 'their heads to the heavens/sky' and 'برند' is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive/optative form of 'بردن' used here in a poetic narrative sense 'they raise' or 'they carry'. The clause means 'all the Romans raise their heads to heaven' (i.e., show pride/arrogance).
segments.s704-g110: 'سخنها' (their speeches/words) is the topic; the poetic preposition 'ز' = 'from' in 'ز اندازه' yields the idiom 'ز اندازه بیرون' = 'beyond measure/limit'. 'بیرون برند' again uses 'برند' (3rd-plural) to indicate that their words 'go out beyond' — i.e., 'their words exceed proper bounds'.
segments.s704-g111: 'نخواهم' is the first-person present negative 'I do not want/wish'. The conjunction 'که' introduces the subordinate clause expressing the undesired outcome. 'رومی بود سرفراز' places the predicate 'سرفراز' (victorious/proud) after the copula 'بود' (here a subjunctive/optative 'be'), yielding 'that a Roman be proud/triumphant.' This is the standard classical Persian pattern after verbs of wishing.
segments.s704-g112: 'به ما' is the affected object 'upon us' / 'to us'. 'برکنند' is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they remove/drive away/dispossess' (used after the negative wishing verb to state the feared result). 'اندرین' = 'in this' (poetic), 'جنگ ناز' = 'the proud/vainglorious battle' (compounded noun + epithet). Together: 'that they may drive us out in this vainglorious battle.'
segments.s704-g249: چواز is a fused temporal particle (چو + از) meaning 'when/since'; پیش here means 'earlier' (time). بدخواه is the agent 'ill‑wisher/enemy'. برداشتش is the simple past verb برداشت 'took/removed' with the enclitic object -ش 'him'. The clause is a temporal subordinate ('since an ill‑wisher had taken him').
segments.s704-g250: به آسانی is an adverbial phrase 'with ease / easily' (به here functions adverbially). آورد is the simple past 'brought' and the conjunction و links to بگذاشتش, where گذاشت (past) plus enclitic -ش means 'put/placed him down'.
segments.s704-g251: بدو is the dative clitic 'to him'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (here reported speech) with خسرو as the subject (postverbal, common in classical word order). که introduces the reported question/clause; نام تو چیست is a verbless interrogative clause where چی is 'what' and ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s704-g252: همی‌گفت is an iterative/imperfective expression 'kept saying' (همی- marks continuous/habitual action). چندی و چندی is a repetitive/time expression 'for a while' (two occurrences intensify duration). گریست is the past 'wept/wailed', describing what he did during that time.
segments.s704-g253: فرشته is the subject 'the angel'. بدو is the dative 'to him'. گفت is the past 'said'. نامم سروش is a nominal copular clause with the copula omitted (classical Persian often omits هست/است): نامم = 'my name' (or 'my name is'), followed by the predicate سروش (the proper name).
segments.s704-g254: چو here means 'when'. ایمن شدی is a temporal subordinate 'when you become/become safe' (شدی is second‑person past/perfect of شدن used to mark state). دور باش is an imperative 'be distant / keep away'. از introduces the object of separation 'from', and خروش means 'uproar/tumult' — the angel's instruction is a direct imperative.
segments.s704-g255: کزین پس is a compound adverbial 'from this on / henceforth'. شوی is second‑person singular of شدن 'you will become / you shall be'. بر جهان expresses scope/jurisdiction 'over the world' (بر + noun), and پادشا is 'king/monarch' — together 'you shall be king over the world.'
segments.s704-g256: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'it should not be / you must not' (expressing prohibition/obligation). که introduces the subordinate clause; باشی is subjunctive/present 'that you be'. جز از پارسا literally 'except from a pious (person)' → idiomatically 'be none but a pious man.' The construction forbids any status other than piousness.
segments.s704-g97: همی‌راند is the epic progressive past (همی + verb) meaning 'was riding/was driving'; it governs the following noun گردوی, here an epithet meaning 'walnut-colored (steed)'. تا here marks the endpoint 'as far as / up to', and نزد شاه means 'in the presence of the king' (نزد = 'before/near'). Word order places the verbal phrase before the goal phrase ('تا نزد شاه').
segments.s704-g98: ز + آهن شده روی is a poetic way to say 'whose face had become of iron' — ز (poetic 'from/of') + آهن 'iron' + شده 'become' + روی 'face'. جنگی سیاه is a nominal phrase 'a warlike/warrior, black' (جنگی used adjectivally/epithetically and سیاه 'black'), describing the person; the predicative chain places the result ('آهن شده روی') before the attributive description.
segments.s704-g99: برو آفرین is a fixed exclamatory phrase 'bravo/well done' (برو here appears in formulaic use); کرد is the past tense 'made/uttered' and takes خسرو as its subject. به مهر is an adverbial phrase 'with kindness/affection' (به + مهر). The clause therefore reads literally 'Khosrow made the exclamation “bravo” with affection.'
segments.s704-g100: که introduces the content/wish clause. پاداش بادت is an optative formula: پاداش 'reward' + بادِ-ت (باد = optative 'may it be' with the enclitic second-person marker -ت) = 'may the reward be yours'. ز گردان سپهر is a poetic genitive/agent phrase 'from the rotating/troop-like host of the heavens' (ز = 'from/of', گردان = 'troop/host', سپهر = 'sky/heavens').
segments.s704-g101: فرستاده خسرو به شاپور is a genitive + prepositional phrase: 'the messenger of Khosrow to Shapur.' کس is an indefinite appositive 'a certain man / someone' that introduces the person being specified.
segments.s704-g102: که begins a relative clause modifying کس ('who'). موسیل راباش is an older word order where the object/place (موسیل, Mosul) precedes راباش ('let it be' / 'may it be'), an optative/imperative-like formula meaning 'let Mosul be ...'. فریادرس is the predicate noun 'succour/rescuer'. Thus the clause says 'who proclaimed/said, “Let Mosul be a rescuer (succour).”'
segments.s704-g103: بکوشید is past-tense plural 'they strove/attempted'. تا here introduces purpose/result 'so that / in order that'. پشت پشت is a reduplicated adverbial phrase meaning 'one after another / back-to-back / in succession' (literal 'back back'). آورید is the subjunctive/jussive 'they might bring / they could bring', completing the purpose clause.
segments.s704-g104: مگر introduces an exception 'unless'. بخت روشن = 'bright/clear fortune' (بخت 'fortune/fate', روشن 'bright/clear'). به مشت آورید is an idiomatic object+verb: به مشت 'into the fist' (i.e. into one's grasp) + آورید 'bring/seize' — 'unless they could seize clear fortune in their hand.' The verb is in the same subjunctive/optative mood as before.
segments.s704-g185: 'کنون' is an initial adverb meaning 'now'; 'هیچ دل را' is an object phrase ('هیچ' = 'any/no', 'دل' = 'heart') marked by the definite-object marker 'را'. 'مدارید' is a 2nd-person-plural jussive/imperative-form verb meaning 'do (not) treat / do not make', and 'تنگ' is an adjective complement meaning 'narrow/tight' that completes the verb phrase ('do not make the heart tight'). Word order is adverb + object + verb + adjective, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s704-g186: 'که' introduces a causal clause here ('for/because'); 'آمد' is a simple past 3rd-sg. verb 'has come'. The pronoun-clitic expressing 'me' appears split in the token list ('م' + 'را' where 'را' is the object marker); together they render 'to me' (i.e. 'has come upon me'). 'روزگار درنگ' is a noun phrase 'a time/fortune of delay' (روزگار = fate/time, درنگ = delay/tarrying), functioning as the subject or circumstance of 'آمد'.
segments.s704-g187: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing 'من' (I) together with two weapons 'گرز' (mace) and 'چوبینه' (wooden cudgel/club). 'بدنشان' is a possessed noun 'their bodies' (بدن + clitic -شان). The phrase is elliptical: the full verb is supplied by the following clause, so this listing functions as the actor/agent(s) or what will be used on the enemies.
segments.s704-g188: 'شما' is the 2nd‑person-plural subject 'you'; 'رزم سازید' is a 2nd‑person-plural jussive/imperative verb phrase literally 'make/prepare battle' (سازید here = 'make/prepare/engage in'). 'با' is the preposition 'with/against' and 'سرکشان' is a plural noun meaning 'the rebellious/headstrong ones' (poetic plural). The clause gives the instruction addressed to 'you'.
segments.s704-g189: 'شما چارده یار' literally 'you (are) fourteen companions' — Persian often places the vocative/subject before the numeral and noun. 'و ایشان سه تن' parallels it: 'and they (are) three persons' where 'تن' is a counting word meaning 'persons'. This is a numeric contrast between the groups.
segments.s704-g190: 'مبادا که' is a conventional negative optative/precautionary phrase meaning 'may it not be that / lest'; it introduces a clause of fear. 'بینید' is the 2nd‑person‑plural present/subjunctive 'you see/meet'; 'هرگز' is the adverb 'ever/never' modifying the verb. 'شکن' is a substantive ('breaker' or 'one who breaks' — here idiomatically 'defeat' or a destructive foe). Together the clause expresses a wish/hope that they never encounter such a breaker.
segments.s704-g191: 'نیاطوس' is a proper name (subject or agent); 'با' is the preposition 'with'; 'لشکر رومیان' is a compound 'the army/host of the Romans' (لشکر = army, رومیان = the Romans/Byzantines). The whole phrase indicates accompaniment or command (i.e. Niyatous together with the Roman army).
segments.s704-g192: 'ببستند' is past 3rd‑person plural 'they closed / shut / surrounded'; in classical/poetic usage it denotes surrounding or blocking. 'ناچار' functions adverbially ('necessarily / compelled / inevitably'); 'یکسر' is an intensifier 'entirely / completely'; and 'میان' indicates location 'in the midst / among / between'. The verb precedes these adverbial/locative elements, a normal poetic order.
segments.s704-g145: جز از + noun phrase = 'except from ...'. 'خواست یزدان' is a genitive phrase 'the will/desire of God'. نباشد is a negative third-person form meaning 'there is not / there should not be' (classical/poetic usage) and here functions as the predicate; سخن ('speech/word') is the noun being negated. Word order is poetic: the negated predicate (نباشد) appears before the noun it governs (سخن).
segments.s704-g146: چنین بود = 'it was thus' (چنین as a sentential adverb, بود = past/copula). تا + بود introduces a temporal clause 'as long as / while ... was', so تا بود چرخ کهن = 'while the ancient sky/heavens existed'. چرخ کهن is a poetic noun phrase (چرخ often = sky or the wheel of fate).
segments.s704-g147: اندرون = 'inside/within' (locative). 'کشته' is a past participle functioning nominally ('being killed' / 'the killed one'); بهتر بود = 'was better' (comparative copula). برزم is an archaic/poetic locative/adverb (here marking place or situation) placed before اندرون for metrical/poetic effect; the overall clause means 'it was better to be killed within ...'.
segments.s704-g148: که here introduces the clause of comparison ('than'). در خانه‌ات = 'in your house' (در + خانه with the second-person singular possessive enclitic -ات). بنده مهتر = 'a bende (servant) who is mehtar' — noun + adjective/title (مهتر = important/elder/chief); بود is the past/copula 'was'. The clause compares the preferable outcome to the state described here.
segments.s704-g149: نگهدار من = 'my guardian' (noun + genitive possessor after the noun: نگهدار + من). بود باید is a poetic inversion of باید بود ('must be' / 'ought to be'); here it yields 'must be' or 'ought to be'. به جنگ is a locative phrase 'in/for battle'.
segments.s704-g150: بهنگام = 'timely, at the proper moment'. جنبش = 'movement' or 'a stirring/advance'. نسازم is the first-person negative of سازم ('I do/make') formed with the negative prefix ن- here meaning 'I will not make/do'; it combines with درنگ ('delay') as its object, so نسازم درنگ = 'I will not cause any delay' / 'I will not hesitate.' The clause therefore expresses resolution not to delay a timely movement.
segments.s704-g151: همه = 'everyone/all' (subject). هم‌زبان here is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'with one voice' or 'of the same tongue' (indicating unanimity). آفرین خواندند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: آفرین ('bravo/acclamation') + خواندند (past plural of خواندن) = 'they cried/cheered "bravo!"' (خواندند is a past action 'they called/sang/uttered').
segments.s704-g152: ورا = 'to him' / 'him' (object pronoun placed before the predicate). شهریار زمین is a nominal phrase 'king of the land' (شهریار = king, زمین = land/realm) serving as the predicate nominative. خواندند (past plural) here means 'they called/declared (him) [such]'; so the clause = 'they called him king of the land.'
segments.s704-g265: برآنم is a stative first-person expression meaning 'I am resolved' (the -م marks 1st person). که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). جنگم is 1st‑person present (جنگ + ـم) meaning 'my fighting / I fight'; کنون is an adverb 'now' placed after the verb. با + noun marks the opponent/companion ('with'); پریست is a poetic/archaic noun here referring to beings associated with pari/Peris (mythical fairies) or those connected to them — taken as the adversary. Word order is subject (implicit I in برآنم) → complement clause introduced by که → verb phrase giving action and instrument/partner.
segments.s704-g266: برین is بر + این (contracted) meaning 'upon this'; تخت تیره is a noun phrase 'gloomy/ominous throne' with تیره qualifying تخت. بباید is the modal 'one must/it is necessary' (old/persianized form of باید with an extra ب-), followed by گریست (the infinitive/old-form of گریستن) to express necessity: 'it must be lamented' or 'one must weep.' The clause is impersonal: 'on this throne there must be lamentation.'
segments.s704-g267: نیاطوس is the proper name (subject). زان روی is a compact causal/locutional phrase: زان ('for that') + روی (here functioning idiomatically as 'on account / therefore'), so together 'for that reason'. بر کوهسار is a locative prepositional phrase 'on the mountain/ hillside'. The order places the causal/locative phrase after the name for poetic emphasis.
segments.s704-g268: همی‌خواست is a progressive/iterative past form 'he wished/was seeking' (همی‑ prefix marks continuous or customary action). از marks source or the person from whom something is sought ('from'). دادگر is an epithet meaning 'the just one' or 'just ruler' (agent). زینهار is a noun 'a warning / admonition' (object of خواست). The construction means 'he sought/asked a warning from the just man.'
segments.s704-g269: خراشید is a past tense verb 'she scratched / tore' (3rd person). مریم is the subject (name) placed after the verb for poetic order. دو رخسار خویش is the direct object 'her two cheeks' (دو numeral + noun + possessive); خویش is written as خوی + ش here, forming the possessive 'her/own.' The verb may be literal or figurative (gesture of grief).
segments.s704-g270: ز is the preposition 'from / out of' (poetic variant). تیمار means 'care/solicitude' and introduces the cause: 'out of care.' جفت here means 'mate/spouse' (i.e., paired one). جهاندار is an honorific noun 'ruler / lord of the world' (a sovereign). خوی + ش again form the possessive 'her own' referring back to Maryam. Together: 'out of care for her mate, her world-ruler.'
segments.s704-g271: سپه is the noun 'army/host' (subject). بود is the past copula 'was/stood' (here narrative past). برکوه و هامون are two locatives 'on the mountain and on the plain' joined by و. وراغ is a terse/poetic adverbial/compound emphasizing extent or spread (the exact etymology is uncertain in the glossary), functioning as an extension like 'and everywhere/around.' The clause describes the army's positioning over different terrains.
segments.s704-g272: دل رومیان is a genitive phrase 'the hearts of the Romans.' ز (here joined with the following و in the manuscript as زو) is a poetic preposition 'from / because of / by it' referring back to the army; this shows cause. پر از is the idiom 'full of' (پر 'full' governed by از 'of'). درد and داغ are the nouns 'sorrow/pain' and 'burning grief' respectively, joined by و. The whole means 'the Romans' hearts were filled by it with pain and grief.'
segments.s704-g33: The initial نه negates the whole clause: 'نه این باشد ...' = 'this is not ...'. این باشد is a copular phrase ('this is' / 'this would be') with باشد in classical/subjunctive/indicative sense. آیین پرمایگان is the nominal predicate 'the custom/practice of the nobles' (آیین = custom/practice; پرمایگان = the great ones/nobles). Word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis: negation + demonstrative + copula + predicate.
segments.s704-g34: همی is a classical/perfective‑continuative particle (emphatic/ongoing) attached to the verbal complex. تن is the direct object meaning 'person/man' here; بکشتن is the infinitive/verbal noun 'to kill'; دهی is the present/subjunctive/imperative form of دادن used here with the infinitive to express causing/inflicting (i.e. 'to cause to be killed' / 'to give (someone) to be killed'); رایگان modifies the whole verbal idea 'for nothing/for free'. The phrase has poetic order: object + infinitive + verb + manner (رایگان) and yields 'to kill a person and give him away for nothing.'
segments.s704-g35: بدو is the dative clitic/adverb 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past verb 'said' (classical narrative past); شاپور is the speaker (subject). The following کای is a vocative particle 'O' and دیوفش is the epithet/name being addressed. Word order 'بدو گفت شاپور' (dative + verb + subject) is common in epic prose/poetry for emphasis.
segments.s704-g36: سرخویش in this compact poetic form functions as a reference to the person (literally 'his head / himself' in older usage) — the one to be acted upon; دربندگی is a compound noun 'into captivity / bondage'; کرده is the perfect participle 'having made' (or 'made'); کش here is an imperative/verb meaning 'pull/seize/drag' (poetic command). The cluster thus describes a command to seize/drag the person and put him into captivity; the order is compressed and characteristic of epic diction.
segments.s704-g37: ازین نامه is the prepositional phrase 'from this letter' (از + این), placed first for topicality. کی is the interrogative 'who'; بود is the past copula 'was'; نام ونشان ('نام و نشان') is a linked nominal phrase 'name and mark / name and seal' serving as the predicate. The clause asks who was named/identified in that letter.
segments.s704-g38: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/which'. گویی here functions as a present-tense speech verb 'you say' / 'you are saying' (or 'it seems you say') in classical style; کنون means 'now'; پیش is 'before/in the presence of'; گردنکشان is a substantive 'neck-cutters' (figuratively 'rebels' or 'proud/executor types'). Together: 'that you now say before the rebels.'
segments.s704-g39: گرانمایه is an epithet 'precious/esteemed' placed before the title; خسرو here denotes the sovereign (the king) as subject. بشاپور is the recipient formed with the prepositional/recipient prefix بـ- + شاپور (i.e. 'to Shapur'); گفت is the past narrative verb 'said'. The whole segment names speaker, addressee and the speech act.
segments.s704-g40: که introduces reported content 'that'. آن نامه is the subject 'that letter'. با رای او is a prepositional phrase 'with his counsel/plan' (با + رای + او) meaning 'in accordance with his opinion'. بود جفت is an archaic/poetic copular phrase: بود = 'was' and جفت (literally 'paired') here means 'matched/accorded' — so 'the letter matched his counsel.'
